answer:
Short version from the research: **yes, “interesting work” — usually studied as intrinsic motivation, autonomy, skill variety, challenge, meaningfulness, person–job fit — is one of the more reliable predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.** Salary matters, but after financial comfort, marginal pay differences tend to be much weaker predictors than people expect. Evidence on “regret” specifically is much thinner.

## 1. Interesting / intrinsically motivating work is strongly linked to job satisfaction

A large body of organizational psychology research supports the **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more satisfying and motivating when they involve:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task identity — seeing a whole piece of work through,
- task significance — the work matters,
- feedback,
- challenge / problem solving.

Classic and meta-analytic evidence: **Hackman & Oldham; Fried & Ferris 1987; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**. The Humphrey et al. meta-analysis, covering hundreds of studies, found that motivational job characteristics are strongly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and performance.

For your case, “interesting work” probably maps especially to:

- skill variety,
- intellectual challenge,
- autonomy,
- learning,
- meaningful technical contribution.

Those are not minor perks; they are central predictors of job satisfaction.

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors

Another relevant literature is **person–environment fit / person–job fit**. The question is: does the role fit your interests, abilities, values, and preferred way of working?

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that person–job fit is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction,
- organizational commitment,
- lower intent to quit,
- better performance.

This is probably highly relevant. If Google gives you unusually good fit — intellectually interesting, strong colleagues, autonomy, research depth — that is a large positive factor. If Anthropic gives you poorer fit in the core work but better fit in leadership/growth, then the comparison is not simply “interesting vs salary”; it is “technical-interest fit vs leadership/growth fit.”

## 3. Pay matters, but its relation to job satisfaction is relatively weak

There is direct evidence on pay and job satisfaction. A meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010** found that pay level has a positive but modest correlation with job satisfaction. Roughly, people who are paid more are somewhat more satisfied, but the relationship is much weaker than many people assume.

This does not mean salary is irrelevant. It matters more when:

- you are financially constrained,
- the raise changes your housing/location/family options,
- it changes your sense of fairness or status,
- it creates financial independence or optionality.

But for someone already highly paid at Google, the marginal utility of a “bit higher salary” is likely much smaller than the effect of changing daily work quality.

This is consistent with broader subjective well-being research: **Kahneman & Deaton 2010; Killingsworth 2021; Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023**. Income is positively related to life satisfaction and often affect, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic: a 20% raise is not psychologically comparable to moving from insecurity to comfort.

## 4. Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are especially important

Self-Determination Theory — **Deci & Ryan; Gagné & Deci 2005; Van den Broeck et al. 2016** — argues that well-being at work depends heavily on satisfaction of three psychological needs:

- **autonomy**: I have agency over my work;
- **competence**: I am growing and using my abilities;
- **relatedness**: I work with people I respect and feel connected to.

Interesting research work often satisfies autonomy and competence. Leading a small team may also satisfy autonomy, competence, and relatedness — if you actually enjoy mentoring, strategy, hiring, and coordination. But if management mostly means meetings, bureaucracy, and less technical depth, it may reduce competence-satisfaction in the domain you care about most.

So the key research-backed question is not “Is leadership good?” but: **will the new role satisfy your needs for autonomy, mastery, meaning, and social connection better or worse than your current one?**

## 5. Meaningful work predicts satisfaction, but can also have risks

Meta-analyses on meaningful work — e.g. **Allan et al. 2019** — find strong associations between meaningful work and job satisfaction, engagement, and life satisfaction.

However, meaningful or mission-driven work can also increase overwork or tolerance of bad conditions. Studies of “calling” orientations, e.g. **Bunderson & Thompson 2009**, show that people may accept lower pay, worse boundaries, or heavier burdens when they see work as a calling.

This is relevant in AI labs. If Anthropic’s mission feels highly meaningful to you, that could compensate for less interesting day-to-day work. But if the daily work is substantially less engaging and the environment is more intense, mission alone may not be enough.

## 6. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Job satisfaction is not isolated from general happiness. Meta-analytic work, e.g. **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** and later work by **Bowling et al.**, finds a moderate-to-strong relationship between job satisfaction and life satisfaction.

Causality runs both ways — happier people tend to perceive work more positively, and better work improves life — but the relationship is real enough that daily work quality should not be treated as a narrow career variable. For many people, it affects general well-being.

## 7. Evidence on “regret” is much less direct

I do not know of strong research directly answering: “Do people regret leaving a more interesting job for more money/leadership?” That exact counterfactual is hard to study.

Related findings:

- Long-run regrets often concern **inaction or missed opportunities** more than actions: **Gilovich & Medvec 1995; Roese & Summerville 2005**.
- Career and education are among common regret domains.
- But this does not imply “take the Anthropic offer.” It could mean people regret not taking growth opportunities; it could also mean they regret not pursuing work that fit their interests.

So on regret, the literature gives weaker guidance. It suggests people often regret untried paths, but not which path is better.

## 8. A research-grounded interpretation for your specific choice

Based on the evidence, I would not frame the decision as:

> higher salary + team lead vs more interesting work

but rather as:

> Which option gives better expected daily satisfaction, growth, autonomy, meaning, and future optionality?

Some specific implications:

### If Anthropic is only slightly less interesting

Then the leadership opportunity may dominate. Management/leadership can create new forms of interest: mentoring, setting direction, building a team, influencing strategy. If that is something you have long wanted, it may satisfy psychological needs that your current role does not.

### If Anthropic is substantially less interesting day-to-day

The evidence would make me cautious. Daily task quality is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction. A modest salary increase is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in intrinsic interest, especially if you are already well compensated.

### If the leadership role gives you real autonomy

That matters a lot. “Leading a small team” is valuable if it means real agenda-setting, hiring influence, mentoring, technical direction, and ownership. It is less valuable if it means being a first-line coordinator with less research depth and more meetings.

### If Google can offer leadership soon

Then the comparison changes. If you can plausibly get team leadership at Google while keeping the interesting work, that may be a better fit. It may be worth explicitly exploring internally before deciding.

## 9. Practical way to use the research

I would score both roles on dimensions that the literature repeatedly finds important:

| Factor | Google | Anthropic |
|---|---:|---:|
| Intrinsic interest of daily work | ? | ? |
| Autonomy | ? | ? |
| Learning / mastery | ? | ? |
| Meaning / mission fit | ? | ? |
| Quality of colleagues | ? | ? |
| Manager support | ? | ? |
| Ability to lead / mentor | ? | ? |
| Workload / stress | ? | ? |
| Long-term optionality | ? | ? |
| Compensation impact on actual life | ? | ? |

The highest-weight factors, based on research, are probably: **daily work quality, autonomy, fit, manager/team quality, growth, and workload**. Salary is important, but if the raise is modest relative to your current compensation, it should probably not dominate.

## Bottom line

The research broadly says:

- **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful, well-fitting work is strongly associated with job satisfaction and well-being.**
- **Pay matters, but marginal pay increases have weaker and diminishing effects, especially at high income levels.**
- **Leadership can be a major positive if it increases autonomy, growth, influence, and relatedness — but not if it mainly replaces intrinsically interesting work with coordination burden.**
- **There is little direct evidence on regret for this exact tradeoff.**

So if Anthropic is “less interesting” but still genuinely engaging and gives real leadership, it could be a very good move. If it is materially less interesting in the day-to-day work and the salary increase is only modest, the research would generally favor staying with the more intrinsically rewarding role — unless the leadership path itself is a major source of meaning for you.

answer:
Short answer: **yes—research generally finds that the content of the work itself, especially autonomy, skill use, challenge, meaning, and person–job fit, is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction. Pay matters, but once you are already well-paid, a “bit higher salary” is usually predicted to have a smaller effect on well-being than a large change in day-to-day work quality.**  

The caveat is that “leading a small team” may itself be interesting work if it gives you autonomy, impact, mentoring, strategy, and growth. So the right comparison is not “interesting research vs salary”; it is “what will my actual weeks feel like in each role?”

## 1. Work content is a major driver of job satisfaction

A large literature under the **Job Characteristics Model**—Hackman & Oldham; later meta-analyses by Fried & Ferris, Humphrey et al.—finds that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill variety**
- **Task significance / meaning**
- **Feedback**
- **Task identity**
- **Complexity / problem-solving**
- **Opportunities to learn**

These are basically the academic versions of “interesting work.” Meta-analyses usually find moderate-to-large relationships between these characteristics and job satisfaction, engagement, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions.

A useful summary: **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, a major meta-analysis of work design, found that motivational job characteristics—autonomy, task significance, feedback, complexity, information processing, problem solving, skill variety—were consistently associated with higher job satisfaction and motivation.

So “interesting work” is not a soft or secondary factor in the research. It is close to the center of what predicts satisfaction.

## 2. “Work itself” often beats pay as a predictor

In job-satisfaction facet research—e.g. the Job Descriptive Index literature—the “work itself” facet is usually among the strongest correlates of overall job satisfaction. Pay is usually weaker.

A representative result: **Kinicki et al. 2002**, reviewing Job Descriptive Index research, found that satisfaction with the work itself and supervision tend to relate more strongly to overall job satisfaction than pay satisfaction does.

This does not mean pay is irrelevant. But empirically, people’s day-to-day experience of the work tends to matter a lot.

## 3. Pay matters, but raw pay level has a surprisingly modest relationship with job satisfaction

A directly relevant meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.” They found that actual pay level has only a modest correlation with job satisfaction—roughly around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction is more strongly related to job satisfaction, but that is partly about fairness, expectations, comparison, and whether one feels valued.

For subjective well-being more generally, income has diminishing marginal returns. The famous **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** paper found emotional well-being rising with income up to a point, though later work by **Killingsworth 2021** and **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers 2023** refined that: well-being often continues rising with income, but roughly logarithmically and not equally for everyone.

For your situation, the important implication is: if both jobs already pay very well, then a **small salary increase** is not, on average, expected to produce a huge increase in experienced well-being—unless it changes something material like financial security, family options, risk tolerance, or freedom.

## 4. Person–job fit and interest fit matter

There is also a strong person–environment fit literature. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** meta-analyzed person–job/person–organization fit and found that person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction and negatively associated with intent to quit.

Vocational-interest congruence research—e.g. **Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000**, later work by **Nye et al.**—finds that when people’s interests match their work environment, they tend to be more satisfied and somewhat more stable in their careers. The effect sizes are not always huge, but they are consistent.

So if the Google role is genuinely much better matched to your intellectual interests, that is a meaningful predictor.

## 5. Meaningful work has very large associations with well-being at work

Another adjacent literature is on **meaningful work**. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay 2019** meta-analyzed outcomes of meaningful work and found strong positive relationships with job satisfaction, engagement, organizational commitment, and life satisfaction, and negative relationships with burnout and turnover intentions.

This matters because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical. A role can be intellectually interesting but not meaningful, or meaningful but not technically fascinating. Ideally you want both. If Anthropic’s work is less technically interesting but feels more meaningful or higher-impact to you, that could compensate. If it is less interesting and not more meaningful, that is a warning sign.

## 6. Boredom/underchallenge is not neutral

Research on job boredom and underemployment generally finds negative relationships with well-being, engagement, and retention. Boredom at work is associated with lower job satisfaction and higher turnover intentions, and in some studies with burnout-like symptoms or psychological strain.

So “less interesting” is not merely the absence of a perk. If it means you will often feel underchallenged, disengaged, or unable to use your best skills, that can have a real cost.

## 7. Regret: less direct evidence, but some relevant findings

There is less direct research of the form: “People chose a less interesting but higher-paid job; did they regret it 10 years later?” That exact causal question is hard to study.

But some adjacent findings are relevant:

- **Roese & Summerville 2005** found that major life regrets often involve education and career. People regret domains where they had opportunity and where alternate paths were imaginable.
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** found a common pattern: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions. That could cut both ways here: you might regret leaving fascinating work, or you might regret never trying leadership.
- Affective forecasting research suggests people often overweight salient, easily measurable features—money, status, title—and underweight repeated daily experience. The commuting literature is a classic example: people often accept longer commutes for better jobs/houses, but long commutes reliably predict lower well-being, and people seem not to fully adapt. See **Stutzer & Frey 2008** on the “commuting paradox.”

So the regret literature does not give a clean answer, but it suggests two things:  
1. Daily lived experience matters more than people predict.  
2. Untried personally meaningful opportunities can become regrets.

In your case, “leading a small team is something I always wanted to do” is therefore not trivial. That could be a real intrinsic motive, not just status.

## 8. The evidence-based way to frame your decision

Based on the research, I would not ask only:

> Which job is more prestigious / better paid / more interesting in the abstract?

I would ask:

1. **Actual weekly time use:**  
   What fraction of your week will be spent doing things you find absorbing? Research, design, mentoring, meetings, admin, coordination, writing, hiring, planning?

2. **Autonomy:**  
   Will the Anthropic role give you more control over direction, methods, people, and priorities? Autonomy is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction.

3. **Skill growth:**  
   Will you develop a valuable new skill—technical leadership, management, strategy—that you genuinely want? Growth and competence matter.

4. **Meaning/impact:**  
   Is the less interesting work more meaningful to you? If yes, that may compensate. If no, less so.

5. **Person–job fit:**  
   Are you temperamentally excited by management? Some people want leadership in the abstract but dislike the actual calendar: 1:1s, hiring, conflict, performance management, alignment, process.

6. **Reversibility:**  
   If you try leading a team and dislike it, can you return to IC/research work? Option value matters. A reversible experiment is different from a permanent move away from research.

## Bottom line from the literature

If the choice were purely:

> much more interesting work + already high salary  
> vs.  
> much less interesting work + slightly higher salary

then the research would generally predict that the more interesting job is more likely to support job satisfaction and probably overall happiness.

But your case has a third factor: **leadership**, which may itself satisfy autonomy, competence, impact, and identity needs. If leading the team would be genuinely intrinsically motivating—not merely a title—then Anthropic could still be the better fit despite less interesting technical content.

The strongest research-based heuristic is: **optimize for the quality of your repeated daily work experience, not just the offer attributes.** In the literature, daily autonomy, challenge, meaning, fit, and good collaborators usually matter more than modest differences in pay.

answer:
Yes. The closest research terms are **intrinsic motivation**, **job characteristics**, **person–job fit**, **meaningful work**, and **self-concordant goals**. The short version is:

> Across many studies, the “work itself” — autonomy, intellectual challenge, skill use, meaning, fit with interests, and opportunity to grow — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and is meaningfully related to life satisfaction. Pay matters, but actual pay level is usually a much weaker predictor of job satisfaction once basic needs/security are met.  
>   
> However, “leading a small team” could itself be an intrinsically valuable feature if it gives you autonomy, impact, mentoring, and identity-relevant growth. The key question is whether the Anthropic role is less interesting only technically, or less intrinsically rewarding overall.

Some relevant research results:

---

## 1. Job content is a major predictor of job satisfaction

The classic literature is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. It identifies features such as:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  
- meaningfulness of work  

as drivers of internal motivation and job satisfaction.

Meta-analyses generally support this. For example:

- **Fried & Ferris, 1987**, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, large meta-analysis of work design

The broad finding: jobs with more autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity are substantially associated with higher job satisfaction, motivation, and engagement. Correlations with satisfaction are often in the rough range of **r ≈ .30–.50**, depending on the job feature.

For someone in research engineering, “interesting work” probably bundles several of these: intellectual complexity, skill use, autonomy, novelty, and meaningful progress.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is very important

Another highly relevant literature is **person–job fit**: how well the job matches your abilities, interests, needs, and values.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, “Consequences of individuals’ fit at work”

They found person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intention to quit. The correlation between person–job fit and job satisfaction is often around **r ≈ .50+**, which is large by organizational psychology standards.

So if “Google role = much better fit with my curiosity and technical interests” and “Anthropic role = lower fit, despite more money/status,” the literature would treat that as a serious factor, not a luxury consideration.

---

## 3. Intrinsic motivation tends to beat purely extrinsic motivation for well-being

Self-Determination Theory — **Deci & Ryan** — argues that people do better when work satisfies three psychological needs:

- **autonomy**: I have agency  
- **competence**: I am growing and effective  
- **relatedness**: I feel connected to others  

Work that is interesting, self-directed, and mastery-oriented tends to satisfy these needs.

Relevant sources:

- **Ryan & Deci, 2000**  
- **Gagné & Deci, 2005**, self-determination theory and work motivation  
- **Van den Broeck et al., 2016**, meta-analysis/review of basic psychological needs at work

The general finding: satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness predicts job satisfaction, engagement, lower burnout, and better well-being.

This is especially relevant because your decision is not simply “interesting vs money.” The Anthropic role might give you more **autonomy, competence growth in leadership, and relatedness through managing people**. If so, it may score well on intrinsic motivation even if the technical work is less interesting.

---

## 4. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap.

Meta-analytic work on meaningful work, e.g.:

- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work

finds meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower depression/withdrawal. Associations with job satisfaction and engagement are often moderate to large.

So if one role feels much more meaningful or intellectually alive, that is a real predictor of happiness, not just a preference quirk.

---

## 5. Actual salary has a surprisingly modest relationship with job satisfaction

A key meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction”

They found actual pay level is only weakly related to job satisfaction — roughly **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction is more related to job satisfaction than objective pay, but objective salary itself is not one of the dominant predictors.

This does **not** mean money is irrelevant. Money matters a lot when it affects security, freedom, location, family constraints, retirement optionality, etc. But between two already well-paid research/AI roles, a “bit higher salary” may have less effect on daily happiness than job content, autonomy, stress, team quality, and fit.

Broader income/well-being research is mixed but points similarly:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point; life evaluation kept rising.  
- **Killingsworth, 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**: experienced well-being continues to rise with income for many people, but roughly logarithmically and with modest marginal effects at high incomes.

So: extra salary is good, but the marginal subjective-well-being value of an extra compensation bump at Google/Anthropic levels is probably not huge unless it materially changes your life options.

---

## 6. Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are meaningfully linked

This matters because you asked not just about job satisfaction but happiness.

Meta-analyses such as:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**  
- **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010**

find job satisfaction and life satisfaction are correlated around **r ≈ .40-ish**. The relationship is likely bidirectional: happier people like their jobs more, but better jobs also contribute to life satisfaction.

So choosing a more satisfying job is plausibly choosing a happier life, not merely a happier 9-to-5.

---

## 7. Regret research: less direct, but suggestive

There is less direct evidence on “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paid/managerial one?” I do not know of a clean study that answers exactly that.

But some adjacent findings are relevant.

### Long-run regrets are often about missed opportunities

Research by **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** and later regret studies suggest that over the long run, people often regret **inactions** more than actions — things they did not try, risks they did not take, paths they did not explore.

Also, **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that education and career are among the most common domains of regret.

This cuts both ways in your case:

- You might regret leaving deeply interesting research for a less interesting leadership role.  
- But you might also regret not trying team leadership when you had the chance, especially since you said it is something you have always wanted to do.

So the regret literature does not simply say “stay with interesting work.” It says people often regret not pursuing identity-relevant opportunities.

### Extrinsic goals are less associated with well-being

There is also research on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations:

- **Kasser & Ryan, 1993/1996**  
- **Dittmar, Bond, Hurst & Kasser, 2014**, meta-analysis on materialism and well-being

People who prioritize extrinsic goals such as wealth, image, and status more heavily tend to have lower well-being on average. This does not mean money/status are bad, but if the Anthropic offer is attractive mainly because of salary/prestige, the evidence would be cautious. If it is attractive because of leadership, impact, mission, autonomy, or growth, that is different.

---

## 8. Leadership may help or hurt depending on what it replaces

The “lead a small team” part is important. Management/leadership can improve well-being if it increases:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- sense of impact  
- mentoring/relatedness  
- career growth  
- identity fit  

But it can reduce well-being if it mainly increases:

- meetings  
- coordination load  
- emotional labor  
- accountability without control  
- less time doing the craft you love  
- chronic interruption  

This maps onto the **Job Demands–Resources** model:

- **Demerouti et al., 2001**  
- **Bakker & Demerouti, 2007**

Job resources like autonomy, support, feedback, and growth predict engagement. Job demands predict strain and burnout, especially when resources are insufficient.

So “less interesting but leading a team” could be good or bad depending on whether leadership is a genuine source of intrinsic motivation for you or just an appealing title.

---

## Evidence-based interpretation for your situation

Based on the research, I would frame the choice like this:

### Reasons the Google role may predict greater happiness

If it has:

- much more intellectually interesting work  
- stronger person–job fit  
- more research autonomy  
- better day-to-day flow/mastery  
- more meaningful technical contribution  
- lower managerial burden  

then the literature would predict higher job satisfaction from those features. These are among the strongest known predictors.

### Reasons the Anthropic role may still be better

If the small-team lead role gives you:

- a long-desired leadership path  
- more autonomy and ownership  
- stronger mission alignment  
- meaningful mentorship  
- greater impact  
- a self-concordant growth opportunity  

then the “less technically interesting” work may be offset by other intrinsically rewarding factors. The evidence would not classify this merely as “money/status”; leadership can satisfy deep motivational needs.

### Reasons to discount the salary bump somewhat

If the salary difference is only “a bit higher” and both jobs already meet your financial needs, research suggests the salary difference should probably not dominate the decision. Actual pay is usually a weaker predictor of job satisfaction than work content, fit, autonomy, and meaning.

---

## A compact research-based decision heuristic

Ignore title and compensation for a moment and compare the two roles on these dimensions:

1. **Autonomy** — where will you have more real agency?  
2. **Competence/mastery** — where will you grow in a direction you value?  
3. **Relatedness** — where will you have better collaborators/mentees/manager?  
4. **Meaning/impact** — where will the work feel more worth doing?  
5. **Daily activities** — what will a normal Tuesday feel like?  
6. **Fit** — which role better matches your interests and identity?  
7. **Reversibility** — if you try leadership and dislike it, can you return to research?  
8. **Regret minimization** — which path would feel more like an untried important life direction if you declined it?

The literature most strongly supports choosing the role with better **fit, autonomy, meaningful work, and intrinsically rewarding daily activities**. It gives weaker support to choosing based on a modest salary increase alone. But your desire to lead a team is a real intrinsic factor, not merely extrinsic ambition.

answer:
There is research, though not much that directly answers “Google research role vs. Anthropic team-lead role.” The closest evidence comes from job-satisfaction, work-design, vocational-fit, and regret literatures.

## Short version

Across many studies, **intrinsic job qualities** — interesting work, autonomy, skill use, learning, task significance, and fit with one’s interests — are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work well-being. **Pay matters**, but above a comfortable level its marginal association with job satisfaction is usually much smaller than the association of job content, autonomy, and meaning.  

That said, “leading a small team” is not merely an extrinsic perk. Leadership can increase autonomy, impact, learning, status, relatedness, and career optionality — all of which are also linked to satisfaction. So the real comparison is not “interesting work vs. salary/title,” but:

> Will the new role give you enough autonomy, mastery, meaning, growth, and day-to-day enjoyment to compensate for less technical/research interest?

The research would generally warn against taking a job that is **substantially less intrinsically engaging** mainly for a modest salary increase. But if the team-lead role satisfies a deep long-term motive — leadership, impact, responsibility — it may still score well on the same factors that predict happiness.

---

## 1. Job content is a major predictor of job satisfaction

A central framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman and Oldham. It argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

These map closely onto “interesting work.”

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson, 2007** found that motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity — have substantial relationships with job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. The typical correlations are often in the **.3 to .5** range, which is large by organizational-psychology standards.

In plain terms: people tend to be more satisfied when their work is intellectually engaging, autonomous, meaningful, and lets them use valued skills.

This is one of the most robust findings in organizational psychology.

---

## 2. Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is surprisingly modest

A useful comparison is the meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, and Rich, 2010**, on pay and job satisfaction. They found that actual pay level has only a **modest** relationship with job satisfaction — roughly around **r ≈ .15**.

That does not mean money is irrelevant. It matters more when:

- income is low or financially stressful;
- pay is perceived as unfair;
- compensation affects autonomy, location, childcare, retirement, etc.;
- the salary difference is very large;
- money is tied to status or validation.

But for already well-compensated professionals, a “somewhat higher salary” often predicts less extra happiness than people expect.

This is consistent with broader subjective well-being research: income is positively related to life satisfaction, but the effect is usually closer to **log income** than raw income. A 10–20% raise at an already high income is usually not psychologically equivalent to escaping financial insecurity.

Relevant papers:  
- **Judge et al., 2010**, pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis  
- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**, income and emotional well-being  
- **Killingsworth, 2021**, income and experienced well-being  
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**, reconciliation showing income continues to matter for many, but with heterogeneous effects

---

## 3. Interest fit and “person-job fit” predict satisfaction and persistence

The vocational psychology literature studies whether people are happier and more successful when their work fits their interests.

Meta-analyses on **person-environment fit** and **vocational interest congruence** generally find that fit predicts:

- job satisfaction;
- commitment;
- lower turnover intentions;
- sometimes performance and persistence.

The effects are not perfect or deterministic, but they are consistent.

Classic and relevant findings include:

- **Assouline & Meir, 1987** and **Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg, 1993**: interest congruence is positively related to satisfaction, often with correlations around **.2**.
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**: person-job fit and person-organization fit are strongly related to job satisfaction and organizational commitment.
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012**: vocational interests predict performance and persistence beyond some other individual-difference measures.

So if by “interesting” you mean “this work deeply fits my abilities and curiosity,” the evidence says that is not a luxury factor. It is predictive of satisfaction and persistence.

---

## 4. Meaningful work and autonomy are especially important

“Interesting” work often overlaps with **meaningful work**, **autonomy**, and **mastery**.

Self-determination theory, associated with **Deci and Ryan**, argues that well-being is strongly supported by satisfaction of three psychological needs:

- autonomy — feeling volitional and self-directed;
- competence — feeling effective and growing;
- relatedness — feeling connected to others.

Workplace meta-analyses, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al., 2016**, support the idea that need satisfaction at work is associated with higher well-being and lower burnout.

Meaningful-work research finds similar results. Meta-analytic work by **Allan et al.** and related researchers finds that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, and life satisfaction.

So the question is not only “Is the work interesting?” but:

- Will I have autonomy?
- Will I keep learning?
- Will I feel competent?
- Will the work feel meaningful?
- Will I like the people and culture?
- Will I have control over my time?
- Will I be doing things I respect?

These are all empirically important.

---

## 5. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction and health

Job satisfaction is not separate from life satisfaction. Meta-analyses find a moderate-to-large relationship between job satisfaction and overall subjective well-being.

For example:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989** found a substantial correlation between job and life satisfaction.
- **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010** reviewed evidence that job satisfaction and subjective well-being are reciprocally related.
- **Faragher, Cass & Cooper, 2005** found job satisfaction is associated with mental and physical health outcomes, especially burnout, anxiety, depression, and self-esteem.

This matters because a job is not just a compensation stream; it is a large fraction of waking life. If the new role is less interesting every day, that can matter a lot.

---

## 6. But leadership can itself be intrinsically satisfying

You mentioned that leading a small team is something you have always wanted to do. Research would not classify that merely as “status” or “extrinsic reward.”

Leadership can increase several factors associated with satisfaction:

- autonomy;
- responsibility;
- task significance;
- social connection;
- mentoring;
- identity development;
- sense of impact;
- career growth.

So if the Anthropic role is less interesting technically but more interesting interpersonally/organizationally, the tradeoff may be less negative than “interesting vs. boring.”

However, management also often increases:

- meetings;
- coordination costs;
- ambiguity;
- people problems;
- accountability without full control;
- emotional labor;
- reduced maker time.

The job-demands/resources literature — e.g. **Bakker & Demerouti’s Job Demands-Resources model** — would predict that leadership is good when it comes with resources: autonomy, support, clarity, influence, good team, sane workload. It is bad when it mainly adds demands without control.

So the empirical question is: does this leadership role increase your autonomy and meaning, or mostly your administrative load?

---

## 7. Regret research: people often regret missed opportunities, but evidence is indirect

There is less direct research on “Do people regret leaving the more interesting job for more money/title?”

But general regret research gives a few relevant patterns.

**Gilovich and Medvec, 1995** found that people often regret **actions** more in the short term, but **inactions** more in the long term. Over time, people commonly regret opportunities they did not try.

**Roese and Summerville, 2005** found that common life regrets cluster around domains with perceived opportunity: education, career, romance, parenting, self-development. Career is one of the major regret domains.

This could cut both ways:

- You might regret not trying leadership when you had the chance.
- You might regret leaving unusually interesting research work.
- You might regret optimizing for salary/title if the daily work becomes dull.
- You might regret staying too comfortable and not testing a desired identity.

The regret literature suggests that **reversibility and optionality** matter. If you can try the team-lead path and plausibly return to research, regret risk is lower. If leaving means giving up a rare research trajectory that is hard to regain, regret risk is higher.

---

## 8. Evidence-based synthesis for your case

Based on the research, I would weight the factors roughly like this:

### Very important for long-term satisfaction

- day-to-day intrinsic interest;
- autonomy;
- learning/mastery;
- meaningfulness/impact;
- fit with your identity and strengths;
- quality of manager/team/culture;
- workload and control over time.

### Important but often over-weighted

- moderate salary increases when already highly paid;
- prestige;
- title;
- company brand.

### Potentially very important in your case

- opportunity to lead, if it is a genuine intrinsic goal rather than just status.

The most research-consistent framing would be:

> Do not trade away highly interesting work merely for a modest salary increase.  
> But do consider trading some technical interest for a role that gives you autonomy, growth, leadership, meaning, and a chance to become the kind of person you want to become.

A useful practical test is to ignore salary and title for a moment and ask:

1. Would I still be excited about the Anthropic role if compensation were identical?
2. Would I still be interested if the title were “project coordinator” rather than “team lead”?
3. What fraction of my week would be spent on work I find energizing?
4. Am I drawn to the actual work, or to what the offer symbolizes?
5. If I stayed at Google, is there a credible path to leadership soon?
6. If I joined Anthropic and disliked the less-interesting work, could I return to research?
7. Which regret seems more likely in five years: “I left amazing research too soon” or “I never tried leading when I had the chance”?

The research does not give a deterministic answer, but it does suggest that **interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is not a soft preference — it is one of the strongest known predictors of work satisfaction**. Salary should matter, but unless the increase materially changes your life, it probably deserves less weight than the day-to-day content of the work and the kind of growth the role enables.

answer:
There is research, though not much that cleanly answers “interesting Google research role vs slightly higher-paid less-interesting Anthropic team-lead role.” The closest evidence comes from job-design, person–job fit, salary–well-being, and regret literatures.

## Short version

The research generally says:

1. **Intrinsic job content — interesting, challenging, meaningful work with autonomy — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Salary matters, but its marginal effect is usually small once you are already financially comfortable**, especially if the difference is “a bit higher” rather than life-changing.
3. **Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction** quite substantially.
4. **People often regret missed career opportunities**, but the regret literature does not specifically say people regret choosing “interesting work” over “more money.”
5. A “less interesting” job can still be satisfying if it gives you **autonomy, growth, responsibility, impact, and good social context**. Leading a small team may satisfy some of the same psychological needs as interesting technical work.

So the research-based framing would be: don’t compare “interesting work” only against salary. Compare the full roles on **autonomy, challenge, learning, meaning, social environment, workload/stress, career growth, and pay**. If the Anthropic role is only better on salary/title but much worse on intellectual engagement/autonomy, research would predict a real risk of lower satisfaction.

---

## 1. Interesting/challenging/autonomous work is strongly related to job satisfaction

A central line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman and Oldham. It argues that jobs are more motivating when they provide:

- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- autonomy,
- feedback.

These map pretty closely onto what people often mean by “interesting work”: using valued skills, solving nontrivial problems, seeing impact, having room to make decisions.

A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson, 2007** found that motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task significance, feedback, variety, complexity — are substantially associated with:

- higher job satisfaction,
- higher internal motivation,
- higher commitment,
- lower turnover intentions,
- better performance.

The correlations are not tiny; many are in the roughly **0.3–0.5** range, which is large by organizational psychology standards.

Similarly, research on **self-determination theory** — Deci and Ryan; Gagné and Deci — finds that work is more satisfying when it supports:

- **autonomy**: “I have real agency”;
- **competence**: “I am growing and using my abilities”;
- **relatedness**: “I work with people I respect and feel connected to.”

Interesting research work usually satisfies autonomy and competence very well. Leading a team may satisfy autonomy, competence, and relatedness — but only if the role is designed well.

---

## 2. Person–job fit matters a lot

Another relevant literature is **person–job fit**: whether your abilities, interests, and values fit the role.

A meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, and Johnson, 2005** found that person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction and negatively associated with intention to quit. Approximate corrected correlations were large, with person–job fit and job satisfaction around the **0.5** range in many analyses.

There is also research on **vocational interests**, often using Holland’s RIASEC model. Meta-analyses such as **Nye, Su, Rounds, and Drasgow, 2012** find that interest congruence predicts satisfaction, performance, persistence, and lower turnover, though the correlations are usually more modest than broad person–job fit.

The implication: if “much less interesting” means “less aligned with the kind of problems I naturally care about,” that is not a trivial factor. It is one of the things that reliably predicts satisfaction and persistence.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but less than people often expect once basic needs are met

There is a large literature on income, pay, and happiness.

A useful organizational psychology result is from **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, and Rich, 2010**, a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction. They found that absolute pay level has a **surprisingly small** relationship with job satisfaction. Roughly:

- pay level vs job satisfaction: around **r = 0.15**;
- pay level vs pay satisfaction: somewhat higher, but still not enormous.

In plain language: higher pay helps, but it is usually not one of the strongest predictors of whether someone likes their job.

On general well-being, **Kahneman and Deaton, 2010** famously found that emotional well-being increased with income up to a point, after which the relationship flattened, while life evaluation kept rising. Later work by **Killingsworth, 2021**, and **Killingsworth, Kahneman, and Mellers, 2023** revised this: well-being often continues to rise with income, but the effect is roughly logarithmic and varies across people. In other words, going from $80k to $160k matters more than going from $400k to $480k.

For someone already highly paid in big tech/research, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely, on average, to compensate for a large drop in daily job interest — unless that extra pay has special meaning for you: financial independence, family obligations, immigration/security concerns, charitable giving, status, etc.

Also, pay is not only about absolute dollars. Research on pay satisfaction suggests people care about **fairness, comparison, and recognition**. Being underpaid relative to peers can hurt satisfaction even if absolute pay is high.

---

## 4. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

This is important because one might ask: “Even if the work is less interesting, maybe the rest of life compensates.”

A meta-analysis by **Bowling, Eschleman, and Wang, 2010** found a substantial relationship between job satisfaction and subjective well-being/life satisfaction. The job–life satisfaction correlation is often around **0.3–0.4+** depending on measures.

This does not prove that job satisfaction causes all of life satisfaction — personality and life circumstances affect both — but longitudinal evidence supports some spillover in both directions.

The practical meaning is simple: work quality matters. If you spend a large fraction of waking hours on work, the daily experience of that work is not easily isolated from the rest of life.

---

## 5. Meaningful work is also a major predictor

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are distinct but often overlap. A role may be intellectually fascinating but not meaningful, or meaningful but technically dull.

Meta-analytic work on meaningful work — e.g. **Allan et al., 2019** — finds that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction,
- work engagement,
- organizational commitment,
- life satisfaction,
- lower withdrawal intentions.

The relationship between meaningful work and job satisfaction is often quite large.

For your decision, this matters because the Anthropic role might be “less interesting” in a narrow technical sense but more meaningful if you care deeply about the mission, influence, or leadership responsibility. Conversely, it might be less interesting and less meaningful, in which case the salary/title premium would be doing most of the work.

---

## 6. Regret research: people often regret missed opportunities, but evidence is indirect

There is less direct evidence on “Do people regret staying in the more interesting job instead of taking more money?”

The closest research is on life regrets. **Roese and Summerville, 2005** reviewed regret studies and found that common major regret domains include:

- education,
- career,
- romance,
- parenting/family,
- self-improvement.

Career regrets are common. Their “opportunity principle” says people tend to regret areas where they perceive they had meaningful opportunities and did not take them.

Also, **Gilovich and Medvec, 1995** found a common pattern: in the short term people often regret actions, but over the long term they more often regret **inactions** — things they did not try.

Applied carefully, this could cut both ways:

- You might regret leaving uniquely interesting work for a less engaging role.
- Or you might regret not trying the leadership path you “always wanted to do.”

The regret literature does not say “always take the opportunity.” It says that foregone personally meaningful opportunities are a common source of later regret. If leading a team is genuinely important to your identity/career, that deserves weight independent of salary.

---

## 7. Management/leadership can be satisfying, but only under the right conditions

Leading a small team is not just a title change; it changes the job’s psychological profile.

Potential positives:

- more autonomy,
- more influence,
- more task significance,
- mentoring and relatedness,
- broader scope,
- career capital.

Potential negatives:

- more meetings,
- less deep work,
- less direct research,
- conflict management,
- ambiguity,
- accountability without control,
- administrative load.

The job-demands/resources literature — e.g. **Bakker and Demerouti’s Job Demands–Resources model** — suggests that high demands are not automatically bad. “Challenge demands” can be energizing if paired with autonomy, support, and resources. But “hindrance demands” — bureaucracy, role conflict, lack of control, politics — predict burnout and dissatisfaction.

So for the Anthropic role, the key research-relevant question is not just “manager or not?” It is:

- Will I have real authority or just responsibility?
- Will I still do intellectually serious work?
- Is the team’s mission meaningful to me?
- Is the work less interesting but still challenging?
- Is the manager/org supportive?
- Are the demands challenge-like or hindrance-like?

---

## My research-based synthesis

If we translate the research into your situation:

### Staying at Google is favored by the evidence if:

- the work is genuinely much more interesting;
- you have autonomy and good collaborators;
- you are still learning rapidly;
- the Anthropic salary increase is modest relative to your current compensation;
- the Anthropic role would reduce deep technical/research engagement a lot;
- the leadership opportunity is attractive mostly as a title, not as day-to-day work.

### Moving to Anthropic is favored by the evidence if:

- leading a team is intrinsically meaningful to you, not merely instrumentally useful;
- the role gives real autonomy, scope, and impact;
- the mission matters to you;
- the “less interesting” work is still challenging and growth-producing;
- the Google role, despite being interesting, lacks growth or future leadership path;
- you would otherwise keep wondering “what if I had tried leading?”

The strongest research-backed warning is: **do not overvalue a modest salary increase relative to daily work quality**. Pay has weaker average relationships with job satisfaction than autonomy, meaningfulness, challenge, and fit.

The strongest counter-warning is: **do not define “interesting work” too narrowly**. For some people, building a team, setting direction, mentoring, and having organizational impact become deeply interesting, even if the technical object-level work is less fascinating.

---

## References worth looking up

- Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. 1976. *Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory.*
- Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. 2007. *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.*
- Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. 2005. *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis.*
- Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. 2010. *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.*
- Bowling, N. A., Eschleman, K. J., & Wang, Q. 2010. *A meta-analytic examination of the relationship between job satisfaction and subjective well-being.*
- Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. 2019. *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*
- Roese, N. J., & Summerville, A. 2005. *What we regret most... and why.*
- Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H. 1995. *The experience of regret: What, when, and why.*
- Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. 2010. *High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being.*
- Killingsworth, M. A. 2021. *Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year.*
- Killingsworth, M. A., Kahneman, D., & Mellers, B. 2023. Work reconciling income–well-being findings.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is a lot of research suggesting that “interesting work” — usually studied as autonomy, skill use, variety, challenge, meaning, and person–job fit — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.** Pay matters, but at already-high income levels the marginal effect of “a bit higher salary” is usually much smaller than the effect of the work itself.  

The caveat: there is much less direct research on the specific counterfactual “I stayed in the more interesting job vs. took the higher-paying/leadership job; did I regret it?” Most evidence is correlational or based on job changes, not randomized career decisions.

## 1. “Interesting work” is a major predictor of job satisfaction

Organizational psychology usually breaks “interesting work” into things like:

- autonomy / control over how you work  
- skill variety and complexity  
- learning and challenge  
- task significance / meaning  
- feedback  
- person–job fit: “this job uses what I’m good at and care about”

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman & Oldham. Meta-analyses generally find that enriched jobs — jobs high in autonomy, variety, significance, feedback, etc. — are substantially associated with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and engagement.

Relevant studies:

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976**, “Motivation through the design of work”  
- **Fried & Ferris, 1987**, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, large meta-analysis of work design

The Humphrey et al. meta-analysis found that motivational work characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and job complexity have moderate-to-large relationships with job satisfaction — often correlations roughly in the **.3–.5** range, depending on the feature. In social science terms, that is large.

So, if by “interesting” you mean “the work is challenging, uses my abilities, gives me autonomy, and feels meaningful,” the literature says that is not a minor perk. It is central.

## 2. Person–job fit and interest fit also matter

Another relevant literature is **person–job fit** and **vocational interest congruence**: people tend to be more satisfied and less likely to leave when their job matches their interests, values, and abilities.

Relevant studies:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis of person–environment fit  
- **Assouline & Meir, 1987**, meta-analysis on vocational interest congruence  
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012**, meta-analysis on vocational interests and performance

Findings: person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction and negatively related to turnover intention. Vocational interest congruence has smaller but reliable associations with satisfaction, persistence, and performance.

One nuance: “interesting” is not only “intellectually exciting.” A job can be less technically fascinating but still fit you if it gives you leadership, autonomy, mentoring, organizational impact, or mission alignment. So the relevant question is not only “which work is more intellectually interesting?” but “which role better fits the kind of work-life I actually want?”

## 3. Meaningful work has strong associations with well-being

There is also a “meaningful work” literature. Meaningful work predicts job satisfaction, engagement, organizational commitment, and lower turnover intentions.

Relevant studies:

- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work  
- **Wrzesniewski et al., 1997**, “Jobs, Careers, and Callings”  
- **Duffy & Dik**, work on “calling” and career well-being

The meta-analytic evidence generally finds meaningful work has strong positive associations with job satisfaction and engagement, and moderate associations with overall life satisfaction.

Caveat: “calling” or meaningful work can also make people tolerate worse conditions or overwork — e.g. the “zookeeper” study by **Bunderson & Thompson, 2009** showed that people with a strong sense of calling may accept sacrifices. So meaning is good, but not if it becomes a reason to ignore bad working conditions.

## 4. Pay matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is surprisingly modest

The research on pay is more mixed. Money clearly matters for security, options, status, family obligations, and life evaluation. But the direct relationship between actual pay level and job satisfaction is usually not huge.

A useful meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction”

They found that actual pay level was only weakly correlated with job satisfaction — around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction correlates more with job satisfaction, but actual salary itself is not one of the strongest predictors.

For general well-being, the income literature says roughly:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.  
- **Killingsworth, 2021**: experienced well-being continues rising with income, roughly logarithmically.  
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**: reconciliation: for many people well-being keeps rising with income, but effects differ across happiness levels.

The key practical implication is: **income effects are usually closer to log income than linear income.** A 10–20% raise at an already high salary is real, but the expected well-being effect is probably modest compared with a large change in day-to-day work quality.

## 5. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Job satisfaction is not isolated from overall happiness. Meta-analyses find a moderate correlation between job satisfaction and life satisfaction.

Relevant studies:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**, meta-analysis of job and life satisfaction  
- **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010**, meta-analytic work on job satisfaction and subjective well-being

Typical correlations are around **.3–.5**. The causal direction is partly both ways: happier people are more satisfied at work, but good/bad work also spills over into life.

So if one job is substantially more satisfying day to day, research would predict that this likely affects overall well-being, not just “work happiness.”

## 6. Leadership is a separate variable

Your case is not just “interesting work vs. salary.” It is also “individual contributor/research work vs. leading a small team.”

The research would suggest evaluating the leadership role on the same dimensions:

- Would it increase autonomy?  
- Would it increase meaning/impact?  
- Would it use skills you want to develop?  
- Would it give you competence growth?  
- Would it improve relatedness/mentoring?  
- Or would it mostly add meetings, coordination burden, ambiguity, and less time doing work you enjoy?

The **Job Demands–Resources** model is relevant here: high demands hurt well-being when not balanced by resources like autonomy, support, clarity, and control.

Relevant work:

- **Bakker & Demerouti**, Job Demands–Resources model  
- **Crawford, LePine & Rich, 2010**, meta-analysis distinguishing challenge vs. hindrance demands

Leadership can be satisfying if it gives challenge, autonomy, influence, and meaning. But it can be draining if it mainly increases workload, role conflict, and interruptions.

## 7. Regret research: career regrets are common, but the evidence is less specific

There is research on regret, but not much directly on “interesting job vs. higher-paying less-interesting job.”

Relevant studies:

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**, “What we regret most… and why”  
- **Morrison & Roese, 2011**, “Regrets of the typical American”  
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**, work on action vs. inaction regrets

Career and education are among the most common domains of regret. Long-term regrets often involve missed opportunities, inaction, and paths not taken. But this literature does not cleanly tell you whether people regret choosing interesting work over salary or vice versa.

A related literature on **affective forecasting** suggests people often overweight salient, easily compared attributes — salary, prestige, title — and underweight repeated daily experience. See:

- **Wilson & Gilbert**, affective forecasting research  
- **Kahneman et al., 2006**, focusing illusion  
- **Nickerson, Schwarz, Diener & Kahneman, 2003**, financial-success goals and later well-being

That does not mean salary/title are unimportant. But it suggests that people can overpredict how much a visible upgrade will affect happiness and underpredict the importance of daily work texture.

## Bottom line from the research

If translated into your situation, the literature would say:

1. **The day-to-day content of the work matters a lot.** Interesting, autonomous, meaningful, skill-using work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.

2. **A modest salary increase probably has a smaller well-being effect, especially if you are already highly compensated.** Pay matters, but marginal returns diminish.

3. **The leadership opportunity could matter a lot if it genuinely satisfies intrinsic motives** — autonomy, growth, mentoring, impact, identity. But if it mostly replaces work you love with coordination you tolerate, that is a real cost.

4. **There is no clean research result saying people systematically regret staying in interesting jobs or regret leaving them.** Regret research is more general: people regret missed growth opportunities, but also regret spending years in roles that did not fit them.

A research-based way to frame the decision would be: don’t compare only “Google interesting work” vs. “Anthropic salary + team lead.” Compare the two roles on the empirically important predictors: autonomy, skill use, learning, meaning, social environment, workload, role clarity, fit, and expected daily tasks. Those variables have much stronger evidence behind them than title or salary alone.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, “interesting/meaningful work” is one of the strongest and most reliable predictors of job satisfaction**, and job satisfaction is substantially related to overall life satisfaction. **Pay matters too, but its marginal effect is usually smaller once you are already financially comfortable**, unless the money changes security, freedom, status/fairness, or future options. The harder part is that “leading a small team” is not just extrinsic—it may itself satisfy autonomy, mastery, impact, and growth needs.

Below are the research findings I’d put the most weight on.

---

## 1. Interesting work / intrinsic motivation is strongly linked to job satisfaction

A large body of organizational psychology supports the idea that people are happier in jobs with:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- opportunities to use strengths,
- challenge without overwhelming stress,
- person-job fit.

This is the classic **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham, supported by later meta-analyses.

**Key results:**

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976; Fried & Ferris, 1987; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**: jobs high in autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback are associated with higher internal motivation, job satisfaction, and performance.
- **Humphrey et al. 2007**, a large meta-analysis, found that motivational job characteristics—especially autonomy and meaningfulness-related features—were among the strongest predictors of satisfaction and motivation.

In plain English: people generally do better psychologically when the work itself is engaging, uses their abilities, gives them agency, and feels worthwhile.

---

## 2. Person-job fit and interest fit matter a lot

There is also direct evidence that fit between a person and the work predicts satisfaction.

**Key results:**

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis of person-environment fit: **person-job fit** is strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. The job satisfaction correlation is often reported around the **r ≈ .5** range, which is large by social-science standards.
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012**, meta-analysis on vocational interests: interest congruence predicts job satisfaction, persistence, and performance, usually with small-to-moderate but meaningful effect sizes.

So if “Google work is much more interesting” means it is a much better fit for your curiosity, strengths, and preferred mode of thinking, that is a serious factor—not a luxury preference.

---

## 3. Meaningful work is also strongly related to well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap. Meaningful work has very strong associations with job satisfaction and engagement.

**Key results:**

- **Wrzesniewski et al., 1997**: people who view their work as a “calling” tend to report higher work and life satisfaction than those who view it merely as a job or career.
- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis of meaningful work: meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

Caveat: there is also literature on the “dark side” of calling—people may tolerate bad conditions, overwork, or exploitation because the work feels meaningful. But in your case, both options likely involve high-status, well-compensated environments, so the main question is probably not exploitation but fit and long-term energy.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but less than people often expect—especially at high income

Research on income and subjective well-being gives a nuanced picture.

**Key results:**

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income up to around $75k/year in 2010 US dollars, then plateaued; life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021**: found no sharp plateau; experienced well-being continues to rise with income, but roughly logarithmically.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**, adversarial collaboration: for most people, higher income continues to predict higher well-being, but the effect is still diminishing; among the least happy people, income appears to plateau around relatively high levels.

The robust conclusion is not “money doesn’t matter.” It is: **money matters a lot when it affects security and freedom; beyond that, each additional dollar tends to matter less.**

For job satisfaction specifically:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, meta-analysis: pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, often around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than absolute pay, and relative/fairness perceptions matter a lot.

So if Anthropic’s salary is “a bit higher” and you are already highly compensated, research would predict that the salary difference alone is unlikely to dominate a large difference in day-to-day work interest—unless the raise changes something concrete: financial independence timeline, family constraints, visa/security concerns, perceived fairness, etc.

---

## 5. But leadership may be an intrinsic reward, not just a title

The Anthropic offer includes leading a small team, which you say is something you have always wanted. That should not be treated as merely extrinsic.

Leadership can increase:

- autonomy,
- influence,
- task significance,
- learning,
- mentoring satisfaction,
- identity/status,
- career optionality.

Those are exactly the kinds of things that research says contribute to motivation and satisfaction.

But leadership can also reduce:

- deep technical/research time,
- flow,
- individual creative work,
- control over your calendar,
- exposure to purely intellectual problems,

and increase:

- meetings,
- people management,
- conflict,
- ambiguity,
- accountability without full control.

The **Job Demands–Resources** literature is relevant here: resources like autonomy, support, and growth opportunities increase engagement; excessive demands, role conflict, and hindrance stressors increase burnout. See **Demerouti et al., 2001; Bakker & Demerouti, 2007; Crawford, LePine & Rich, 2010**.

So the real question is not “interesting work versus leadership.” It is:

> Would the Anthropic role give you a new kind of intrinsically satisfying work, or would it mostly trade intellectually absorbing work for managerial overhead?

That distinction matters a lot.

---

## 6. Job satisfaction substantially spills over into life satisfaction

Job satisfaction is not isolated from the rest of life.

**Key results:**

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**, meta-analysis: job satisfaction and life satisfaction are correlated around **r ≈ .4**.
- **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010**: job satisfaction is consistently related to subjective well-being; evidence suggests reciprocal causation—happy people are more satisfied at work, but work satisfaction also contributes to overall well-being.

So yes, staying in a more satisfying/interesting job is plausibly associated with being happier overall.

But causality is messy: personality, prior happiness, health, family life, and career expectations all matter.

---

## 7. Regret research is less decisive

There is not, as far as I know, a clean study answering:

> “Do people regret choosing interesting work over higher pay/team leadership?”

Regret research is broader.

Relevant findings:

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: career and education are among the most common domains of life regret.
- Regrets often involve perceived missed opportunities, especially when people feel they failed to pursue an important possible self.
- Affective forecasting research—**Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman, Schkade**—shows that people often overestimate how much a single salient factor will affect future happiness.

Implication: people may regret either choice depending on their narrative later.

You could regret leaving Google if you lose rare intellectual work.  
You could regret staying if you keep postponing leadership and later feel you avoided growth.

The literature does not say “always choose interesting work.” It says people often mispredict happiness by overweighting salient symbols—salary, prestige, company brand, title—and underweighting repeated daily experience: actual tasks, people, autonomy, stress, commute, and calendar.

---

## 8. Evidence-based way to evaluate your specific case

Based on the research, I would compare the two roles on these dimensions rather than just salary/title/company:

| Factor | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Intellectual interest | Strongly tied to intrinsic motivation and satisfaction |
| Autonomy | One of the most robust predictors of work well-being |
| Skill use / mastery | Central to person-job fit and motivation |
| Meaning / impact | Strongly linked to engagement and satisfaction |
| Leadership/growth | Could satisfy autonomy, competence, identity |
| Manager / team quality | Social support and supervisor relations matter a lot |
| Stress / workload / ambiguity | High demands can erode well-being |
| Calendar reality | Day-to-day experience predicts happiness better than abstract prestige |
| Compensation / security | Matters most if it changes real constraints or perceived fairness |
| Reversibility / option value | Regret is lower when choices are not one-way doors |

The most research-consistent move would be to get concrete about Anthropic’s actual day-to-day role:

- What percentage of time is technical versus management?
- Will you still do research/engineering yourself?
- What decisions would you actually own?
- What are the team’s goals?
- Is the work “less interesting” permanently, or just less aligned with your current taste?
- Would leadership at Anthropic create future access to more interesting work?
- Could you get a leadership path at Google within a reasonable time?

---

## Bottom line from the literature

If the Anthropic offer is mainly:

> modestly more money + title/status + much less interesting day-to-day work,

then research would generally predict caution. Intrinsic work quality, person-job fit, autonomy, and meaning are major drivers of job and life satisfaction, while marginal compensation at high income usually has diminishing returns.

But if the Anthropic role is:

> less technically interesting, but much higher autonomy, real leadership, greater impact, strong team fit, and a long-desired growth path,

then it may still be the more satisfying job—not because of salary, but because leadership itself can be intrinsically meaningful.

So the research-backed framing is not “interesting work versus money.” It is:

> Which role gives you the better bundle of intrinsic motivation, autonomy, fit, meaning, relationships, manageable demands, and future optionality?

That bundle tends to predict happiness much better than salary alone.
