answer:
Short version: **yes, there is a lot of evidence that intrinsically interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is strongly related to job satisfaction and, to a lesser extent, overall life satisfaction.** The evidence that “somewhat more pay” improves happiness is real but usually much weaker once income is already high. Evidence on “regret” is thinner, but affective-forecasting research suggests people often overweight salient, comparable factors like salary/status and underweight day-to-day experience.

That said, “leading a small team” is not merely an extrinsic perk; it may increase autonomy, impact, learning, and identity fit, which are exactly the kinds of things the research says matter.

## 1. Work content, autonomy, and meaning are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

The closest literature is the **job characteristics / work design** literature.

Classic model: **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

Meta-analytic evidence supports this. For example, **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, a large meta-analysis of work design, found that motivational job features—especially autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity—are substantially associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- intrinsic motivation  
- lower turnover intentions  
- lower strain/burnout  

The broad takeaway: **what you do every day matters a lot**. A job that uses your skills, gives you autonomy, lets you solve hard problems, and feels meaningful is much more likely to be satisfying than one that merely pays more.

This maps pretty directly onto “interesting work,” though the literature usually operationalizes it as autonomy, complexity, skill use, learning, meaningfulness, etc., rather than “interestingness” per se.

## 2. Person–job fit is a large predictor of satisfaction

Another relevant literature is **person–job fit**: whether the work matches your abilities, interests, values, and needs.

A major meta-analysis, **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, found that person–job fit correlates strongly with job satisfaction—roughly around **r = .5** in many estimates—also predicting commitment and lower intent to quit.

That is a big association by organizational psychology standards.

This suggests that if your current Google role is unusually well-matched to what you find intellectually alive, that is not a small thing. Conversely, if the Anthropic role matches another deep preference—e.g. “I want to build and lead a team”—that also matters. The key is whether the new role is a better or worse fit with your core motives, not just whether the topic is less technically interesting.

## 3. Pay matters, but the marginal effect is usually modest at high income

Pay is not irrelevant. But the relationship between pay and job satisfaction is weaker than many people expect.

A commonly cited meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.” They found that pay level has only a modest association with job satisfaction—around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction is somewhat more related, but still not usually as predictive as work content, autonomy, fit, or supervisor quality.

For general happiness, income also matters, but with diminishing marginal returns. The literature has evolved:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, after which it appeared to plateau, while life evaluation kept rising.  
- **Killingsworth 2021**: found experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly log-linearly, with no sharp plateau.  
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers 2023**: reconciled these partly; for most people happiness continues to rise with income, but the effect is modest and differs by baseline happiness.

For someone already at Google-level compensation, the research implication is: **a “bit higher salary” is unlikely, by itself, to dominate a large difference in day-to-day work quality**, unless that salary difference materially changes your life—financial security, family goals, housing, runway, ability to donate, etc.

## 4. Intrinsic motivation predicts performance and satisfaction; extrinsic rewards are not enough

Self-Determination Theory—**Deci & Ryan**—finds that people do better and feel better when work supports:

- autonomy  
- competence  
- relatedness  

Intrinsic goals such as growth, mastery, contribution, and relationships are generally more strongly associated with well-being than extrinsic goals such as money, image, or status. See, for example, **Kasser & Ryan 1993/1996** and later SDT research.

A meta-analysis by **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford 2014** found that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality of performance. Extrinsic incentives can help, especially for quantity or well-defined tasks, but they do not substitute well for intrinsic interest in complex creative work.

For research engineering, where the work is cognitively demanding and quality matters, this is especially relevant.

## 5. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**. Reviews and meta-analyses, e.g. **Allan et al. 2019**, find that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction  
- higher engagement  
- lower withdrawal intentions  
- higher general well-being  

Similarly, **Wrzesniewski et al. 1997** found that people who experience their work as a “calling” tend to report higher work and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career.

This matters because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical. You might find Google’s work more technically interesting, while Anthropic’s work could feel more meaningful, more agentic, or more aligned with your desired identity as a leader. The research would treat both as important intrinsic factors.

## 6. Promotions and leadership are mixed: they can increase autonomy/status, but also stress and loss of craft

The leadership part is important. A move into leading a small team could improve several predictors of satisfaction:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- task significance  
- mentoring/relatedness  
- identity growth  
- career capital  

But management can also reduce other predictors:

- hands-on technical challenge  
- flow  
- uninterrupted deep work  
- sense of individual mastery  
- control over one’s time, depending on meeting load  

Research on promotions is mixed. Some studies find that promotions raise job satisfaction, at least temporarily, partly through pay, status, and perceived advancement. But other work finds promotions can bring more stress and do not necessarily improve health or happiness. For example, **Boyce & Oswald 2012** found that promotions were not clearly associated with better health and were linked to more mental strain in some data.

Leadership itself can be less stressful when it comes with real control—see work related to job control and status, e.g. **Karasek’s demand-control model** and some evidence that higher control buffers stress. But if leadership means responsibility without autonomy, it can be worse.

So the research would not say “leading a team is good” or “management is bad.” It would ask: **does the leadership role actually increase autonomy, competence, relatedness, and meaningful impact, or does it mostly add coordination burden?**

## 7. Regret evidence is limited, but people often mispredict what will make them happy

There is less direct research on “Do people regret choosing interesting work over salary?” But related findings are useful.

Affective forecasting research—**Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman, Schkade, Hsee**, etc.—shows that people often mispredict future happiness. In decisions, people tend to overweight attributes that are:

- easy to compare, like salary  
- status-laden, like title/company  
- salient at decision time  

and underweight repeated daily experiences, such as:

- meetings  
- autonomy  
- manager quality  
- task enjoyment  
- commute  
- interruptions  
- whether the work gives energy or drains it  

The classic idea is the **focusing illusion**: “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.” Salary/title can become focal during a job decision, but day-to-day work experience dominates once you are living the job.

There is also general regret research, e.g. **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**, suggesting that people’s long-term regrets are often about inactions and missed opportunities. That could cut the other way: if leading a team is something you have always wanted and the opportunity is rare, you may later regret never trying it. But that literature is general, not specific to career-interest-vs-salary tradeoffs.

## Research-based synthesis for your situation

If I translate the research, not personal opinion, it would say something like:

1. **Do not overweight the salary bump** if you are already highly compensated. The marginal happiness effect is probably modest unless the money changes concrete life constraints.

2. **Take “less interesting work” very seriously.** Intrinsic work quality, autonomy, skill use, and person–job fit are among the strongest known predictors of job satisfaction.

3. **But treat “leading a small team” as a potentially intrinsic benefit, not just career status.** If leadership gives you autonomy, impact, mentoring, and growth, it may compensate for less technical interest.

4. **The key empirical question is the day-to-day work design.** What fraction of your week would be spent on things you find energizing? Hard technical decisions? Mentoring? Strategy? Hiring? Meetings? Roadmaps? Performance management?

5. **Regret risk depends on reversibility.** If you can return to interesting IC/research work later, trying leadership is less risky. If moving would permanently take you away from the kind of research you love, the research would caution against dismissing that cost.

A compact decision heuristic consistent with the literature:

> Prefer the job with better expected day-to-day autonomy, skill use, learning, meaning, and fit. Let salary break ties, unless the salary difference materially changes your life. Treat leadership as valuable only insofar as it improves those intrinsic dimensions, not merely because it is a promotion.

answer:
Yes—there is relevant research, though not much that directly says “take the more interesting job vs. the higher-paid team-lead job.” The closest literatures are job design, intrinsic motivation, person–job fit, pay and well-being, and career regret.

## Short version

The research generally suggests:

1. **Interesting, intrinsically motivating work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, and performance**, especially in complex/creative jobs.
2. **Autonomy, skill use, learning, feedback, meaning, and social environment** are among the strongest predictors of work well-being.
3. **Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually modest once you are already well-paid**, unless the increase meaningfully changes your life, security, options, or perceived fairness.
4. **Leading a small team could be a major positive** if it gives you autonomy, growth, influence, mentoring, and meaning—but a negative if it mostly means meetings, coordination, and loss of intellectually engaging work.
5. **Evidence on regret is weaker.** People often regret missed opportunities and paths not taken, but research does not cleanly show that people systematically regret choosing interesting work over pay, or vice versa.

So, based on the literature, the key question is probably not “Google vs. Anthropic” or “salary vs. interest,” but:

> In the Anthropic role, would the leadership/mission/autonomy/learning compensate for the lower day-to-day intellectual interest?

If the salary bump is only “a bit higher,” research would usually treat that as less important than the actual daily work experience.

---

## 1. Job content and “interesting work” matter a lot

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman and Oldham. It argues that jobs are more motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

These predict experienced meaningfulness, responsibility, and knowledge of results, which in turn predict motivation and satisfaction.

A major meta-analysis:

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007, Journal of Applied Psychology**  
They reviewed work-design research and found that motivational job characteristics—especially **autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and job complexity**—are strongly related to:

- job satisfaction  
- internal motivation  
- organizational commitment  
- lower turnover intentions  
- sometimes performance  

For a research-engineering job, “interesting work” probably maps onto several of these: skill variety, complexity, learning, autonomy, and feedback from hard technical problems.

So yes: people in more intrinsically rich jobs tend to be more satisfied.

The caveat: much of this evidence is correlational. People with better jobs may differ in other ways. But the consistency across studies is strong.

---

## 2. Person–job fit and interest fit predict satisfaction, but moderately

There is also research on **vocational interests** and person–environment fit, often associated with Holland’s theory of careers.

Meta-analyses such as:

- **Assouline & Meir, 1987**
- **Tsabari, Tziner & Meir, 2005**
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012**

generally find that interest congruence—how well your interests fit your job—is reliably associated with job satisfaction, commitment, and persistence. The correlations are usually **modest**, not enormous, often around the r = .15–.25 range depending on the measure.

Interpretation: fit with interests matters, but it is not the only thing. A job can be less intellectually fascinating but still satisfying if it has autonomy, impact, people you like, good management, and meaningful growth.

---

## 3. Intrinsic motivation is especially important for complex/creative work

For high-skill knowledge work, intrinsic motivation seems particularly important.

A useful meta-analysis:

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014, Psychological Bulletin**  
They looked at intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives across many studies. Intrinsic motivation predicted performance, and was especially important for **quality of performance**, not just quantity. Extrinsic incentives also matter, but often more for quantity, routine effort, or clearly measurable tasks.

For a research engineer, where quality, creativity, judgment, and persistence through ambiguity matter, this suggests that “being genuinely interested” is not just a happiness issue; it may affect how good you are at the work.

This is one reason I would not dismiss “interesting work” as a luxury. In research-heavy roles, it can be central.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but at high income the marginal effect is often smaller

There is a large literature on income and well-being.

A relevant workplace-specific meta-analysis:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**  
They found that pay level is positively related to job and pay satisfaction, but the association is not huge. Roughly, pay correlates only modestly with overall job satisfaction.

Broader well-being research:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that life evaluation keeps rising with income, while emotional well-being appeared to plateau around a certain income level in their data.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** challenged the plateau result, finding that experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly log-linearly.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, well-being keeps increasing with income, but for the least happy subgroup, emotional well-being may plateau at higher incomes.

The practical takeaway is nuanced:

- More money usually helps somewhat.
- But the effect is typically diminishing.
- At already-high tech salaries, a “bit higher” salary is unlikely to dominate daily work quality unless it changes something concrete: financial independence timeline, family goals, visa/security issues, ability to donate, housing, risk tolerance, etc.

So if the Anthropic salary is only modestly higher, the research would usually not say “take it for the money.”

---

## 5. Autonomy may be as important as interestingness

A lot of research points to **autonomy** as one of the most robust predictors of job satisfaction and well-being.

This appears in:

- Job Characteristics Model research
- Self-Determination Theory, e.g. **Deci & Ryan**
- workplace meta-analyses on psychological need satisfaction, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al., 2016**

Self-Determination Theory says people thrive when work satisfies needs for:

- autonomy  
- competence  
- relatedness  

A team-lead role could improve all three:

- more autonomy/influence  
- a new competence frontier: leadership  
- more relatedness through coaching/mentoring  

But it could also reduce autonomy if you become constrained by planning, politics, stakeholder management, or organizational pressure.

So “leading a small team” is not merely an extrinsic perk. It may itself be intrinsically motivating. If you have always wanted to do it, that deserves real weight.

---

## 6. Meaning and mission also matter

Research on **meaningful work** finds strong associations with satisfaction, engagement, and retention.

For example:

**Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes  
Meaningful work is associated with higher job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

If Anthropic’s work feels more important or socially meaningful to you, that could partially or fully offset it being less technically interesting. But if it feels both less interesting and not more meaningful to you personally, then the evidence becomes more favorable to staying where your work is more engaging.

A useful distinction:

- **Interesting work**: “I enjoy thinking about these problems.”
- **Meaningful work**: “I believe this work matters.”
- **Growth work**: “This makes me into the person I want to become.”
- **Autonomous work**: “I have real agency.”
- **Socially good work**: “I like and respect the people; I feel I belong.”

These are correlated but not identical.

---

## 7. Work affects overall life satisfaction substantially

Job satisfaction is not isolated from life satisfaction.

Meta-analyses such as:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**
- **Bowling et al., 2010**

find that job satisfaction and life satisfaction are substantially related, often correlations around .3–.4 or higher depending on methods.

There is debate about causality: happier people may like their jobs more, and good jobs may make people happier. The relationship is probably reciprocal.

But it is safe to say: for someone spending a large fraction of waking hours at work, job quality matters a lot.

---

## 8. People adapt to some things more than others

Affective forecasting research suggests people often mispredict what will make them happy. They overfocus on salient differences and underweight repeated daily experiences.

Relevant ideas:

- **Kahneman’s focusing illusion**: “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.”
- People often adapt to changes in income/status faster than expected.
- Repeated daily frictions—bad commute, bad manager, lack of autonomy, unpleasant meetings—can remain costly.

For your case, this suggests you should focus less on the abstract label of the role and more on the repeated weekly texture:

- How many hours of deep technical/research work?
- How many hours of meetings?
- How much control over direction?
- How much ambiguity/stress?
- How much mentoring/coaching?
- How much interaction with people you admire?
- How much of the work would you voluntarily think about?

Daily experience matters.

---

## 9. Evidence on regret is weaker

There is some regret research, but it is not specific enough to answer your exact question.

Findings include:

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: people’s largest regrets often involve education, career, romance, and parenting.
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: over the long term, people often regret **inactions** more than actions.

This might suggest people regret not taking opportunities. But both options contain possible inaction:

- Staying at Google: you may regret not trying leadership at Anthropic.
- Moving to Anthropic: you may regret leaving unusually interesting research.

So regret research does not strongly settle the issue. It mostly says: pay attention to which option you would later view as the more meaningful missed opportunity.

---

## Research-informed interpretation for your situation

Given your description:

> Google: very interesting work  
> Anthropic: somewhat higher salary, small team leadership, less interesting work

The literature would likely assign high importance to:

1. **Day-to-day intellectual interest**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Growth/skill development**
4. **Meaning/mission**
5. **Quality of colleagues and manager**
6. **Workload and stress**
7. **Pay**, especially if the difference is life-changing; otherwise less

The salary bump alone is probably not enough, according to the research, if the work is meaningfully less engaging.

But the leadership opportunity is different. If leading a small team is genuinely a long-standing intrinsic goal, it may be comparable in importance to “interesting technical work.” The decision may really be:

> Do I currently value technical/research depth more, or leadership growth/autonomy/impact more?

That is a much closer call than salary vs. interesting work.

---

## A practical evidence-based way to evaluate it

I would try to estimate the Anthropic role on the dimensions most supported by the research:

| Dimension | Ask yourself / them |
|---|---|
| Autonomy | Will I choose research direction, or execute someone else’s roadmap? |
| Skill use | Will I still use my strongest technical skills? |
| Skill growth | Will I learn something important and rare? |
| Task significance | Will the work feel important? |
| Feedback | Will I get clear signals of progress? |
| Social environment | Do I trust and admire the people? |
| Manager quality | Will my manager protect focus and autonomy? |
| Workload | Will leadership mean sustainable responsibility or chronic stress? |
| Identity fit | Will I feel “this is my kind of work”? |
| Reversibility | If I dislike it, can I return to research-heavy roles? |

If Anthropic scores high on autonomy, growth, meaning, people, and reversibility, then the lower technical interest may be worth it.

If it scores mainly on salary and title, while the actual work is less interesting and more managerial overhead, the research would lean toward staying with the more intrinsically motivating role.

---

## Bottom line

The research does support the idea that **interesting, intrinsically motivating work is a major determinant of job satisfaction and well-being**. It is not a trivial preference. For complex research/engineering work, it also likely affects performance and long-term career energy.

However, “leading a small team” may itself be an intrinsically motivating form of work, not just an extrinsic perk. The key is whether the Anthropic role gives you more autonomy, meaning, growth, and influence—or merely more responsibility with less interesting substance.

If the salary increase is only moderate, I would not give it much weight relative to the daily work, autonomy, manager/team quality, and the leadership-growth opportunity.

answer:
Short answer from the literature: **yes, “interesting work”/intrinsic work quality is one of the stronger predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being**, usually stronger than pay once pay is already high. But “interesting” should be decomposed: autonomy, learning, skill variety, task significance, feedback, person–job fit, and social environment. A management role can score high on some of these even if the technical topic is less fascinating.

## 1. Intrinsic work quality predicts job satisfaction fairly strongly

The most relevant literature is the **Job Characteristics Model**: Hackman & Oldham; later meta-analyses by Loher et al., Fried & Ferris, Humphrey/Morgeson/Nahrgang.

Jobs tend to be more satisfying when they have:

- **Autonomy** — control over methods, schedule, decisions  
- **Skill variety / complexity** — using and developing multiple capabilities  
- **Task identity** — owning a whole piece of work  
- **Task significance / meaning** — work matters to others  
- **Feedback** — clear signal on whether you’re doing well  
- **Growth/learning opportunities**

Meta-analyses generally find correlations in the **~0.3–0.5 range** between these work-design features and job satisfaction/motivation. That is large by organizational psychology standards.

A useful citation: **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, a large meta-analysis of work design, found motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity, etc. — were substantially related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower withdrawal intentions.

So if by “interesting” you mean “I learn, solve hard problems, have autonomy, and care about the work,” the research says that is not a trivial preference. It is one of the central determinants of job happiness.

## 2. Person–job fit is also a very strong predictor

Another relevant literature is **person–environment fit** or **person–job fit**.

Meta-analysis: **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction — roughly around **r ≈ .5** in many analyses — and also with commitment and lower intention to quit.

For your case, the question is not simply “Google vs Anthropic” or “IC vs manager.” It is:

> Which role better fits what you actually want to do day-to-day?

If your identity and energy come from doing frontier technical research, then a role with less hands-on interesting work may become dissatisfying even if it has more status/pay. If what you really want is to develop people, set direction, make decisions, and multiply a team’s output, then the Anthropic role may be intrinsically interesting in a different way.

## 3. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually smaller at high income

There is good evidence that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the association is modest.

A frequently cited meta-analysis: **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010** found that **pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction**, around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, but raw pay itself is not a huge predictor of overall job satisfaction.

For general well-being, income does matter, but roughly logarithmically: a given percentage increase matters more than a fixed dollar increase, and marginal utility is lower at already-high compensation levels. The Kahneman & Deaton / Killingsworth line of work debates details, but the practical conclusion is similar: **money helps, especially when it reduces stress or changes life options, but at high tech salaries the day-to-day work experience often dominates.**

So if the Anthropic salary difference materially changes your life — e.g. financial independence, family constraints, immigration/security, ability to take future risks — then it deserves real weight. If it is “nice but not life-changing,” research suggests not overweighting it relative to intrinsic work quality.

## 4. Job satisfaction is related to overall life satisfaction

This is not just “work feelings.” Meta-analyses find that **job satisfaction and life satisfaction correlate moderately**, often around **r ≈ .3–.5**. See e.g. **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** and later work by **Bowling et al.**

Causality runs both directions: happier people perceive jobs more positively, and better jobs make people happier. But the link is strong enough that choosing a daily work environment you find meaningful/interesting is plausibly important for broader well-being.

## 5. Promotions/job changes often have a honeymoon effect

There is research on the **“honeymoon–hangover” effect** in job changes: people often become more satisfied immediately after changing jobs, but some of the boost fades as novelty wears off and the real work conditions become salient. See **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy 2005**.

This matters because a higher salary, new title, prestigious company, and “finally leading a team” may create a real initial boost. But the long-run question is what your calendar looks like six months in:

- Are you mostly doing work you find energizing?
- Do you have autonomy?
- Do you like the people?
- Are you learning?
- Are you proud of the work?
- Is the management load energizing or draining?

## 6. Management can be intrinsically rewarding — but it changes the source of interest

The literature does not say “management is better” or “IC research is better.” It says satisfaction comes from need fulfillment: autonomy, competence, relatedness, purpose, growth.

Self-determination theory — **Deci & Ryan**, plus workplace meta-analyses such as **Van den Broeck et al. 2016** — finds that people do better when work satisfies:

- **Autonomy**: I have agency.
- **Competence**: I am good and improving.
- **Relatedness**: I work with people I care about/respect.

A small-team lead role can score very high on these: mentoring, setting agenda, making decisions, seeing people grow. But it can also involve more meetings, conflict management, ambiguity, hiring/performance issues, and less deep technical flow. Whether it is “less interesting” depends on which activities you find intrinsically rewarding.

## 7. Regret research is less direct

There is not much clean evidence like: “people who chose interesting jobs over higher-paying jobs regretted it X% less.” Career-regret research is broader.

Some relevant findings:

- **Roese & Summerville 2005**: education and career are among the most common life-regret domains.
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**: people often regret actions more in the short term, but **inactions** more in the long term.
- Regret is strongest where people perceive they had meaningful opportunity and failed to pursue an important identity/goal.

Applied cautiously: if leading a team is something you have wanted for years, not trying it might become a regret. Conversely, if your core identity is doing unusually interesting research, leaving that for a less interesting managerial path may also become a regret. The regret literature does not cleanly favor one side; it suggests paying attention to which forgone path would feel like abandoning an important self-concept.

## Practical research-based framing

I would evaluate the two roles not as “interesting work vs salary,” but across dimensions that the literature says matter:

| Factor | Research relevance | Your question |
|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic interest / complexity | Strong predictor of satisfaction | Which role gives you more energizing day-to-day work? |
| Autonomy | Very strong predictor | Where do you control roadmap/methods/decisions? |
| Growth | Important for motivation | Is management growth more valuable to you than technical growth right now? |
| Task significance | Strong predictor | Which work feels more consequential? |
| Person–job fit | Very strong predictor | Which role fits your actual strengths and identity? |
| Team/manager quality | Strong predictor | Whom will you work for and with? |
| Workload/stress | Important for burnout | What are hours, pressure, emotional demands? |
| Pay | Positive but weaker at high levels | Does the salary difference change your life/options? |
| Option value | Not always studied directly, but important | Can you return to research/IC work if management is not for you? |

## Bottom line from the research

The strongest evidence says:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is a major driver of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay has a positive but comparatively small relationship with job satisfaction once you are already well paid.**
3. **Person–job fit may matter more than either title or salary.**
4. **A management role can be deeply satisfying if it satisfies autonomy, competence, relatedness, growth, and impact — but it may disappoint if you mostly value deep technical problem-solving.**
5. **Direct regret evidence is weak; regret depends on which path represents an important unrealized identity or opportunity.**

So the research would not say “always stay with the more interesting job.” It would say: be very cautious about giving up a role with high intrinsic interest unless the new role offers comparable intrinsic rewards through leadership, autonomy, impact, growth, or personal fit.

answer:
Short version: **yes, the literature generally supports taking “interesting/meaningful/autonomous work” very seriously**, especially once compensation is already high enough that the marginal salary change does not alter security or lifestyle much. The evidence is much stronger for **job satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and turnover** than for “life regret” specifically. Regret research is more indirect.

Below are the most relevant findings I know.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” shows up in research as autonomy, skill use, variety, meaning, and fit

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: Hackman & Oldham, later meta-analyzed by Fried & Ferris and others. It says jobs are more motivating/satisfying when they have:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill variety**
- **Task identity** — seeing a whole piece of work through
- **Task significance** — feeling the work matters
- **Feedback**
- **Complexity/problem-solving**

Meta-analyses generally find these “motivational job characteristics” have **moderate to large relationships with job satisfaction and intrinsic motivation**.

Relevant sources:

- Hackman & Oldham, 1976, *Motivation through the design of work*
- Fried & Ferris, 1987, meta-analysis of job characteristics
- Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007, meta-analysis of work design

The last one is especially relevant: it integrates many job-design studies and finds that motivational features like autonomy, task significance, complexity, and feedback are strongly associated with satisfaction, motivation, and lower withdrawal.

**Interpretation for your case:** if “interesting work” at Google means real autonomy, challenging research, novelty, skill growth, and intellectual engagement, the literature would treat that as a major predictor of job satisfaction—not a luxury factor.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

Another closely related literature is **person–job fit**: whether the job gives you what you value and uses what you are good at.

A major meta-analysis:

- Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work*

They found that person–job fit is strongly related to:

- Job satisfaction
- Organizational commitment
- Lower intention to quit
- Somewhat better performance

Vocational-interest research finds similar things: people whose work matches their interests tend to be more satisfied and persist longer.

Relevant sources:

- Kristof-Brown et al., 2005
- Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012, on vocational interests and performance/persistence
- Assouline & Meir, 1987, meta-analysis on interest congruence and well-being/satisfaction

**Interpretation:** if the Anthropic role is genuinely less aligned with your intellectual interests, that is a real expected cost. On the other hand, if leading a small team is itself a deep interest/identity fit for you, then the Anthropic role may have a different kind of fit.

---

## 3. Meaningful work is strongly associated with satisfaction and well-being

“Interesting” is not exactly the same as “meaningful,” but they overlap. Meaningful work has been studied a lot.

A meta-analysis:

- Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019, *Outcomes of meaningful work*

Findings: meaningful work is strongly related to:

- Job satisfaction
- Work engagement
- Organizational commitment
- Lower withdrawal intentions
- Some relationship with broader life satisfaction and well-being

Also relevant:

- Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997, “Jobs, careers, and callings”

They found that people who view their work as a **calling** report higher job and life satisfaction, even across different occupations.

**Interpretation:** if your current work feels meaningful and intellectually alive, that is predictive of happiness. If the new role is less interesting but more meaningful because of mission, leadership, or impact, that could compensate.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is often smaller than people expect—especially at high income

For job satisfaction specifically, pay is surprisingly weak compared with work content, fit, autonomy, and relationships.

A useful meta-analysis:

- Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction*

They found that **actual pay level has only a modest correlation with job satisfaction**. Satisfaction with pay matters more than raw pay, but even then it is only one part of overall job satisfaction.

For general happiness/life satisfaction:

- Kahneman & Deaton, 2010: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point in their data, while life evaluation kept rising.
- Killingsworth, 2021: found experienced well-being continues rising with income, roughly logarithmically.
- Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023: reconciled the two; for many people well-being continues rising with income, but the marginal returns are smaller and differ by baseline happiness.

The important practical point: **income effects are roughly logarithmic**. Going from $80k to $120k is psychologically larger than going from $400k to $500k, even though the latter is a bigger absolute raise.

**Interpretation:** if both jobs already pay very well, a “bit higher salary” is probably a weaker predictor of daily happiness than whether you like the work, your manager, your autonomy, and your workload. Pay becomes more important if it changes major life constraints: housing, family security, retirement timing, ability to take risks, etc.

---

## 5. Autonomy is especially important

Self-Determination Theory is very relevant here. It argues that people thrive when work supports:

- **Autonomy** — I have agency
- **Competence** — I am growing and effective
- **Relatedness** — I feel connected to others

Workplace studies generally find that satisfaction of these needs predicts engagement, job satisfaction, and lower burnout.

Relevant sources:

- Deci & Ryan, Self-Determination Theory
- Deci, Connell & Ryan, 1989, workplace autonomy-support study
- Van den Broeck, Ferris, Chang & Rosen, 2016, review/meta-analysis of basic psychological needs at work

There is also economics evidence that people value autonomy highly. For example, self-employed people often report higher job satisfaction despite lower income or longer hours, partly attributed to independence and control.

Relevant source:

- Benz & Frey, 2008, on self-employment, autonomy, and job satisfaction

**Interpretation:** leading a small team could be very good if it increases your autonomy, agency, impact, and sense of competence. But it could be bad if it mostly means meetings, coordination, performance management, and less deep work.

---

## 6. Relationships, manager quality, and workload can dominate both salary and “interestingness”

The literature also says not to reduce this to “interesting work vs salary.” Other major predictors include:

### Manager/team quality

Leader–member exchange and supervisor support are strongly related to satisfaction and commitment.

- Gerstner & Day, 1997, meta-analysis on leader–member exchange
- Dulebohn et al., 2012, meta-analysis on leader–member exchange

### Work–life conflict

Work-family conflict is consistently associated with lower job satisfaction, lower life satisfaction, stress, and burnout.

- Allen et al., 2000
- Amstad et al., 2011

### Job insecurity

Job insecurity is reliably harmful for satisfaction and mental health.

- Sverke, Hellgren & Näswall, 2002, meta-analysis

**Interpretation:** a less interesting job with a great manager, great team, sane hours, and high autonomy may beat an interesting job with chaos and overload. Conversely, a highly interesting job with supportive people is a very strong combination.

---

## 7. Evidence on regret is weaker and more indirect

There is research on life regrets, but it usually does not cleanly answer: “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying job?”

Some relevant findings:

- Roese & Summerville, 2005: people’s biggest regrets often involve education, career, romance, and parenting. Career regrets are common.
- Gilovich & Medvec, 1995: in the long run, people often regret **inactions** more than actions—missed opportunities, paths not taken.
- Research on intrinsic vs extrinsic goals, e.g. Kasser & Ryan, Nickerson et al., suggests that people who heavily prioritize financial success over intrinsic goals often do not end up happier, even when they earn more.

But I would not overstate this. Regret is very path-dependent. Someone might regret not taking the leadership opportunity; another might regret leaving deeply interesting research.

**Best evidence-based framing:** people tend to regret choices that violate their important values or close off salient identities. If “being a technical researcher doing frontier work” is central to you, leaving it may create regret. If “becoming a leader” is central to you, not trying management may create regret.

---

## 8. How I would translate the research to your specific decision

Not as personal advice, but as a research-based decision heuristic:

### The salary increase should probably get limited weight if:

- You are already very well compensated.
- The raise does not materially change your security or freedom.
- The new job is clearly less interesting day-to-day.
- You expect less autonomy, less deep work, or less learning.

### The Anthropic offer should get more weight if:

- Leading a team is not just status but genuinely intrinsically motivating to you.
- The role gives you more autonomy, influence, mentorship, and ownership.
- You expect strong manager/team fit.
- The work is less technically interesting but more meaningful or impactful.
- It opens a path you have repeatedly wanted and cannot easily get at Google.

### The Google role should get more weight if:

- The current work is unusually interesting and hard to replicate.
- You have high autonomy and strong collaborators.
- The Anthropic role would mostly replace research with coordination.
- The salary difference is modest relative to your current income.
- You could pursue leadership internally without giving up the interesting work.

---

## Bottom line

The research does **not** say “always choose the most interesting work.” But it does say that **work content, autonomy, meaning, fit, and relationships are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction**, while **additional salary has relatively small marginal effects once you are already highly paid**.

So if the Anthropic role is “a bit more money + management title + substantially less interesting work,” the literature would make me cautious. If instead it is “less technically interesting but much more autonomy, leadership, ownership, meaning, and identity fit,” then the research could support taking it.

answer:
There isn’t a clean study of “elite AI researcher chooses more-interesting IC job vs less-interesting team-lead job,” but there is a lot of adjacent evidence. The short version is:

**Intrinsic work quality—interesting/challenging work, autonomy, skill use, meaning, fit with interests—predicts job satisfaction and well-being quite strongly. Pay matters, but absolute pay level is usually a weaker predictor, especially once you are already highly paid. However, “leading a small team” may itself be an intrinsic/growth factor, not just status.**

## 1. Interesting/challenging work is one of the stronger predictors of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually decomposed into things like:

- autonomy / control over how you work  
- skill variety and skill use  
- task significance / meaning  
- feedback and learning  
- challenge/complexity  
- fit between your interests and the work

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: Hackman & Oldham, 1976; Fried & Ferris, 1987. Later meta-analyses, especially **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, find that motivational work characteristics such as autonomy, variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity have medium-to-large associations with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and lower withdrawal/turnover intentions.

A related literature on **person–job fit** is also relevant. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** meta-analysis found that person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction — roughly around **r ≈ .5** in many estimates — and also related to commitment and lower intent to quit. Vocational-interest congruence studies usually find smaller but still meaningful effects, often around **r ≈ .2–.3** for satisfaction/persistence.

So if by “interesting” you mean “this job fits my curiosity, lets me use my strongest skills, gives me autonomy, and I keep learning,” the evidence says that is not a luxury variable. It is central.

## 2. Pay matters, but salary level is usually a weaker predictor than people expect

A useful meta-analysis is **Judge et al., 2010, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.”** They found that actual pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction — about **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction/fairness matters more than raw pay, but even there, pay is usually not among the largest drivers compared with work content, autonomy, manager/team quality, and meaning.

On general happiness, the income literature is nuanced:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that higher income improves life evaluation, but emotional well-being showed diminishing returns and an apparent plateau in their data.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** found experienced well-being continues rising with income above that level, but roughly log-linearly: each additional dollar matters less at high income.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of this: for most people, happiness continues to rise with income, but with diminishing marginal returns; for the least-happy group, income may stop helping much past a moderate-high level.

For your case, since both options are presumably already very high compensation, **“a bit higher salary” is likely to have small average well-being impact unless it changes something concrete**: financial security, ability to take risks later, family constraints, immigration/security, perceived fairness, or major wealth upside.

## 3. Meaningful work and intrinsic motivation are strongly linked to well-being

Another relevant body of work is **Self-Determination Theory**: Deci & Ryan; also workplace meta-analytic/review work such as **Van den Broeck et al., 2016**. It emphasizes three psychological needs:

- autonomy  
- competence/mastery  
- relatedness  

Jobs satisfying those needs tend to produce higher motivation, engagement, and well-being.

The **meaningful work** literature points in the same direction. **Allan et al., 2019** meta-analysis found meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. **Wrzesniewski et al., 1997** on jobs/careers/callings found that people who experience their work as a “calling” tend to report higher job and life satisfaction, even across different occupations.

For a research engineer, “interestingness” may be partly about intellectual curiosity, but also partly about meaning: “Do I believe this problem matters?” That tends to be important.

## 4. But “leading a small team” may be intrinsically valuable too

It would be a mistake to classify the Anthropic offer as merely “more salary/status.” Leading a small team can satisfy several high-value factors:

- more autonomy  
- more influence  
- mentoring/relatedness  
- strategic ownership  
- faster growth  
- identity/career development  
- competence in a domain you want to master  

So the real comparison may not be:

> interesting work vs salary/team-lead title

but rather:

> interesting technical/research content vs interesting leadership/growth/autonomy content

Research on promotions is mixed. Promotions often increase job and pay satisfaction, especially if desired, but can also increase stress, meetings, ambiguity, and hours. The job-demands/control literature — e.g. Karasek’s demand-control model and later job-demands/resources models — suggests high demands are more tolerable when paired with high control, support, and meaning. A team-lead role with high autonomy and good support may be energizing; a team-lead role with lots of coordination burden and less actual influence may be draining.

## 5. Job satisfaction is meaningfully related to life satisfaction

Meta-analyses such as **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010** find a substantial relationship between job satisfaction and subjective well-being/life satisfaction. It is not just “work is work, life is life.” For people whose identity and time are heavily tied to work — likely true for many research engineers — job quality spills over into general well-being.

That said, there is also evidence that stable personality/disposition affects job satisfaction, and that people adapt to many changes. So changing jobs will not permanently transform happiness unless the day-to-day work environment really changes in durable ways.

## 6. Regret evidence: less direct, but relevant

I don’t know of a strong direct result saying “people who stayed in more interesting jobs later regretted/didn’t regret it.” The regret literature is more general.

A few relevant findings:

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: education, career, and romance are among the most common domains of major life regret. Regret is common where people perceive they had opportunities.
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions — things they did not try.
- **Wilson & Gilbert** on affective forecasting: people often overestimate how much a single change — salary, title, location, prestige — will affect long-term happiness. Day-to-day experience matters more than headline attributes.

Applied to your situation: if leading a team is “something I always wanted to do,” there is some regret risk in never trying it. But if the actual daily work would be much less engaging, there is dissatisfaction risk in moving.

## Evidence-based takeaways for your decision

The research would not say “always choose the more interesting job.” But it does suggest:

1. **Do not lightly trade away intrinsic interest/fit for a modest salary increase.**  
   Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.

2. **At already-high compensation levels, extra salary usually has diminishing well-being returns.**  
   It matters if it changes financial freedom or risk tolerance; otherwise, probably less than day-to-day work quality.

3. **Treat team leadership as a real intrinsic factor.**  
   If leading a small team gives you autonomy, ownership, mentorship, and growth, it may compensate for a less interesting technical problem.

4. **Focus on the “typical Tuesday,” not the offer headline.**  
   Ask: What will I actually do each week? How many hours of deep technical work? How many meetings? How much autonomy? How good is the manager? How strong is the team? How much impact?

5. **The highest-risk move is to a job that is both less intrinsically interesting and less autonomous.**  
   Higher pay/title usually will not compensate for sustained boredom, low fit, or low control.

6. **The highest-upside move is one where the topic is somewhat less interesting, but the role gives you wanted growth, autonomy, influence, and excellent colleagues.**

So, from the research angle: **if Anthropic is only “less interesting” in topic, but gives you leadership, autonomy, meaning, and growth, it may be a good trade. If it is genuinely less engaging day-to-day and the main advantages are salary/title, the evidence leans toward staying with the more interesting work.**

Internal work motivation is around .35, and performance is at .19. Absenteeism has a weak negative correlation of -.09, and turnover is also around -.19. I should be careful not to provide exact figures.
**Summarizing job satisfaction research**

Research indicates that job quality factors like interest, autonomy, fit, meaning, strong relationships, and manageable demands significantly predict job satisfaction and overall wellbeing. Interestingly, while salary can influence satisfaction, its marginal returns diminish, especially at higher levels. Leadership roles can enhance intrinsic satisfaction through autonomy and growth opportunities. Additionally, regret over untried aspirations may feel significant, but that doesn’t imply salary is the primary concern. I should also consider how “interesting work” could involve technical challenges rather than just novelty.
**Exploring leadership motivators**

In a leadership role, I see potential improvements in areas like autonomy, task significance, social support, and learning opportunities. However, if someone's joy comes primarily from technical or research novelty, their skill utilization might decrease. While a pay raise might not have a significant day-to-day impact at a high base salary unless it boosts financial freedom, company fit, manager relationships, and work hours/stress are crucial as well. Social support is vital, closely linked to job satisfaction and wellbeing.
**Considering career decisions**

The user is interested in comparing jobs, so I think a good approach would be to recommend an evidence-based decision exercise. This could involve rating roles on different factors like intrinsic interest, autonomy, growth, and meaning, while prioritizing daily job design over just salary versus interest. 

Additionally, it could be useful to do a "premortem" by asking which choice they might regret the most. I might also suggest testing leadership appetite without fully switching roles, possibly by taking a temporary role at a company.
**Evaluating income and job satisfaction**

I need to explore the idea that people adapt to income gains, while job characteristics and daily experiences have a more lasting effect. For instance, studies on lottery winners and research from the German SOEP suggest that job satisfaction does increase with wage raises, but adaptation might occur over time.

I might mention that salary fairness is important, as pay satisfaction tends to predict job satisfaction, and Judge et al.'s 2010 meta-analysis reveals that while pay level has a small correlation with job satisfaction, the link between pay satisfaction and job satisfaction is stronger.
**Analyzing job satisfaction factors**

I should note that the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is around 0.15, while pay satisfaction is closer to 0.25 to 0.30. This clearly indicates that pay is a weaker predictor of job satisfaction compared to job content and fit. 

Also, looking at other surveys, like the British Household Panel from Clark in 1998 and 2001, it seems that while all job domains contribute to satisfaction, factors like pay and hours are relatively less important, with job security being the most significant.
**Exploring autonomy and leadership**

I'm thinking about how autonomy and control are some of the strongest characteristics in a job. It seems that taking on leadership roles can enhance autonomy and the sense of significance and learning. However, leading also comes with increased demands and potential conflicts, which I find interesting. If those demands are high but support is low, it could negatively impact well-being, as noted in models like Karasek and JD-R. There's definitely a balance to navigate here!
**Considering regret citations**

I think I need to draft with some citations for context. I want to make sure I'm not quoting unsupported claims about people regretting their careers instead of money. answer:
Yes — there is a relevant literature, though not usually phrased as “interesting work vs. salary.” The closest constructs are **intrinsic motivation**, **job characteristics**, **person–job fit**, **meaningful work**, **autonomy**, and **pay/job satisfaction**.

Short version: **research generally finds that intellectually engaging, autonomous, meaningful, well-fitting work is a large predictor of job satisfaction and well-being; pay matters, but the marginal effect of additional pay is usually much smaller once you are already highly compensated.** However, **leadership** can itself be an intrinsic motivator if it gives you autonomy, growth, responsibility, and impact. Regret research also suggests people often regret **untried opportunities**, especially career ones.

## 1. “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** — Hackman & Oldham, 1970s. It says jobs are more motivating when they provide:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

These map pretty closely onto “interesting work,” especially for research/engineering roles.

Meta-analyses support this. For example:

- **Fried & Ferris 1987** meta-analysis: core job characteristics were substantially associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower withdrawal.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** meta-analysis: motivational work characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity — showed moderate to strong relationships with job satisfaction, motivation, and performance.

The exact correlations vary by characteristic and study, but the general pattern is robust: **jobs that use your skills, give autonomy, provide feedback, and feel significant tend to make people more satisfied.**

For your case, “very interesting stuff” at Google likely corresponds to high **skill use**, **complexity**, and maybe **task significance**. Those are not minor perks; they are central job-satisfaction predictors.

## 2. Person–job fit matters a lot

Another relevant body of work is **person–environment fit** or **person–job fit**: how well the job matches your abilities, interests, values, and preferred way of working.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that person–job fit was strongly related to job satisfaction — roughly one of the strongest relationships in the organizational psychology literature. It also predicted organizational commitment and lower intent to quit.

This is important because “interesting” is partly subjective. A role that is “less interesting” to you may still be prestigious, well-paid, and important, but if it is a worse fit with your intrinsic interests, that predicts lower satisfaction.

Related literature on **vocational interest congruence** — Holland-type interest fit — finds smaller but consistent effects: people tend to be more satisfied and perform better when their work matches their interests.

## 3. Intrinsic motivation is especially important for creative/research work

For routine work, external incentives can predict output quantity. But for creative, ambiguous, research-like work, intrinsic motivation becomes more important.

A useful meta-analysis:

- **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford 2014**: intrinsic motivation predicted performance, especially for complex/quality-oriented tasks. Extrinsic incentives were more strongly associated with quantity of output than quality.

This matters for a research engineer. If your best work comes from genuine curiosity and fascination with the problem, losing that can be costly not only for happiness but also for performance and long-term career capital.

Also relevant:

- **Self-Determination Theory** — Deci & Ryan — finds that satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness needs is associated with well-being, engagement, and persistence.
- In workplace studies, autonomy and competence are repeatedly among the major predictors of engagement and job satisfaction.

So the question is not just “Which job is more interesting?” but:

> Which job better satisfies autonomy, competence/growth, relatedness, and meaning?

Leadership could improve some of these while reducing others.

## 4. Pay matters, but marginally less than people often expect at high income levels

There is a lot of evidence that pay matters, but less strongly than intrinsic/job-design variables, especially once basic needs and security are met.

A very relevant meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction”: pay level had only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. Pay satisfaction mattered more than raw pay, but still not as much as many intrinsic job factors.

In subjective well-being research:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that life evaluation continues to rise with income, while emotional well-being flattened around a threshold. This plateau result has been debated.
- **Killingsworth 2021** found experienced well-being continues to rise with income, with no sharp plateau.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers 2023** reconciled these results: for most people, well-being rises roughly with log income; for the least happy group, it may plateau.

The key point: the relationship is roughly **logarithmic**. Going from low to medium income matters a lot; going from already-high to somewhat-higher income usually has a much smaller effect on day-to-day happiness.

So if Anthropic’s offer is “a bit higher salary” and you are already in a high-compensation Google research role, the direct happiness gain from compensation alone is probably not large — unless it materially changes your financial security, immigration situation, family constraints, giving goals, or ability to retire/take risks later.

## 5. Leadership is not just an extrinsic perk — it can be intrinsically satisfying

Your Anthropic offer is not merely “higher salary but less interesting work.” It includes **leading a small team**, which you say is something you have always wanted to do.

From the research perspective, leadership may increase:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- task significance  
- social connection  
- responsibility  
- learning/growth  
- status and identity  

All of those can contribute to satisfaction.

But leadership may also decrease:

- hands-on technical depth  
- uninterrupted research time  
- direct intellectual engagement  
- time spent on the core problems you find fascinating  
- control over your own calendar  
- emotional energy, due to people management and coordination  

The research does not say “management makes people happier” in a simple way. It says: **greater autonomy, influence, meaning, and growth are good; excessive demands, role conflict, and poor fit are bad.**

So the important question is whether you want leadership because:

1. you genuinely enjoy mentoring, planning, prioritizing, coordinating, hiring, and growing people; or  
2. you like the idea/status/career step of leadership, but would miss deep technical work.

The first predicts better fit than the second.

## 6. Social environment and manager quality are huge

Work satisfaction is not only about work content. Research on job design and burnout repeatedly finds that **social support**, manager quality, fairness, and team climate matter a lot.

Relevant frameworks:

- **Job Demands–Resources model** — Bakker & Demerouti  
- **Demand–Control–Support model** — Karasek and later work  

High demands are not necessarily bad if combined with autonomy, support, and meaning. But high demands plus low control/support predict stress and burnout.

So for Anthropic vs. Google, a research-informed comparison should include:

- Who will be your manager?
- How much autonomy will you actually have?
- How good is the team?
- How intense is the workload?
- Will you be able to shape the work?
- Is the role psychologically safe?
- Is the company/team stable enough for you to do good work?

A less interesting problem in a high-autonomy, high-trust, high-growth environment can beat an interesting problem in a frustrating environment. But if your current Google environment is also strong, that raises the bar.

## 7. Regret research: people often regret missed opportunities

There is less direct research on “people who stayed in a more interesting lower-paid job vs. left for leadership.” But regret research gives one useful warning.

Findings:

- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions.
- **Roese & Summerville 2005**: common life regrets often involve education, career, and missed opportunities. Regret is stronger where people perceive they had a real opportunity.

So if leading a team is a long-standing aspiration and this is a rare concrete opportunity, there is some evidence that **not trying it** could become a source of regret.

But that cuts both ways. You might also regret leaving uniquely interesting work. The regret literature does not say “take the new job.” It says that **unexplored paths with high personal significance are regret-prone**.

A practical implication is: if you stay at Google, you may want to actively create a leadership path there, so that “I never tried leading” does not become the unresolved regret.

## 8. What the research would imply for your specific tradeoff

Based on the evidence, I would frame it this way:

### Salary increase  
Probably positive, but likely a small direct well-being effect if you are already highly paid and the increase is only moderate.

### More interesting work  
Likely a strong positive predictor of job satisfaction, especially in research/creative engineering.

### Leading a small team  
Potentially a strong positive if it matches your real interests and gives autonomy, growth, and meaning. Potentially negative if it mainly removes you from the work you love.

### Company/team/manager/workload  
Could dominate the whole decision. The literature says daily work environment and autonomy/support matter enormously.

## A research-based decision heuristic

Instead of comparing “Google vs. Anthropic” globally, score each role on these predictors:

| Factor | Research relevance |
|---|---|
| Intrinsic interest / intellectual curiosity | Strong predictor of job satisfaction and motivation |
| Autonomy | Very strong predictor of satisfaction and well-being |
| Skill use / competence growth | Strong predictor |
| Meaning / impact | Strong predictor |
| Social support / manager quality | Strong predictor |
| Workload / stress / recovery | Major predictor of burnout |
| Person–job fit | One of the strongest predictors |
| Pay | Matters, but marginal effect is usually modest at high income |
| Leadership fit | Can be highly positive if genuinely desired |
| Future option value | Important for career regret and long-term fit |

The big research-based question is:

> Does the Anthropic role compensate for lower technical interest by giving you more autonomy, growth, meaning, and leadership fit?

If yes, the move may be well-supported by the research.  
If no — if it is mainly “less interesting work + slightly more money + title” — the literature would generally favor staying with the more intrinsically engaging role.

A good compromise, if possible, would be to negotiate for one of these:

- a protected fraction of hands-on technical/research work;
- scope to choose or shape the technical direction;
- a clear path to lead without becoming mostly administrative;
- a 6–12 month review point;
- or, at Google, a serious internal path toward team leadership while keeping the interesting domain.

The evidence does not produce a single answer, but it strongly suggests that **daily intrinsic job quality and fit are not luxuries; they are among the main determinants of work happiness.** Salary is real, but at your likely compensation level, “a bit higher” is probably less important than whether the new role gives you autonomy, meaning, growth, and work you actually want to do every day.
